docs.title: Missing --model_name Parameter
docs.text:
``` ❌ Model Validation Failed: Model Registration Issue

Model 'predictive-analytics' is registered as 'model' instead of 'predictive-analytics'.

This usually means the InferenceService is missing the --model_name parameter.

Fix: 1. Edit charts/hub/templates/model-serving.yaml (or charts/hub/values.yaml if using model loop) 2. Add --model_name parameter to InferenceService args:

args: - --model_name=predictive-analytics # ← Add this line - --model_dir=/mnt/models/predictive-analytics - --http_port=8080

  1. Commit and sync with ArgoCD

See docs/MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_CHECKLIST.md for detailed instructions. ```

docs.title: Model File Not Found
docs.text:
``` ❌ Model Validation Failed: Model File Not Found

KServe logs show: "failed to locate model file for model predictive-analytics under dir /mnt/models/predictive-analytics"

This usually means: 1. Notebook didn't save model to correct path 2. PVC mount is not working 3. Directory path doesn't match model name

Fix: 1. Verify model file exists: oc exec deployment/predictive-analytics-predictor -- ls -lh /mnt/models/predictive-analytics/model.pkl

  1. Check notebook saves to correct path: joblib.dump(model, "/mnt/models/predictive-analytics/model.pkl")

See docs/MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_CHECKLIST.md for detailed instructions. ```

docs.title: ✅ What You Need to Configure
docs.text:
1. Platform: Set spec.modelValidation.platform to your model serving platform
  2. Model Names: List expected model names in spec.modelValidation.targetModels
  3. Downstream Services (optional): Define services that need to reach your models
  4. Test Data (optional): Provide sample input for prediction validation

docs.title: ✅ What the Operator Handles
docs.text:
1. Platform Detection: Automatically uses correct endpoints for your platform
  2. Service Discovery: Finds model pods using platform-specific label selectors
  3. Validation Logic: Runs appropriate checks based on your configuration
  4. Error Reporting: Provides platform-specific error messages

docs.title: ✅ Adding a New Platform
docs.text:
If you need to support a new platform:

  1. Add platform configuration to get_platform_endpoint_config(): python "my-platform": { "list_endpoint": "/api/models", "metadata_endpoint": "/api/models/{model_name}", "predict_endpoint": "/api/models/{model_name}/predict", "port": 8000, "label_selector": "app.kubernetes.io/name={model_name}" }

  2. Use in your NotebookValidationJob: yaml spec: modelValidation: platform: my-platform

  3. Submit PR to operator to add platform configuration permanently

docs.title: Questions for Implementation
docs.text:
1. Where is the operator source code? Need to locate the operator repository to implement these changes
  2. What version should include these enhancements? Suggest v1.0.6 for bug fix, v1.1.0 for CRD changes
  3. Should this be opt-in or opt-out? Suggest opt-in via spec.modelValidation.enabled: true (current behavior)
  4. Should we add Prometheus metrics? Could track validation success/failure rates, model load times, etc.
  5. Integration with ArgoCD health checks? Could use validation results in ArgoCD Application health status
  6. Community feedback on platform support? Which platforms should we prioritize?
  7. Documentation for other projects? Where should we publish examples for different use cases?

docs.title: OpenShift Object Store Setup for Model Serving
docs.text: This guide explains how to set up OpenShift Data Foundation (ODF) with NooBaa for model artifact storage, following the Validated Patterns framework.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. OpenShift Cluster: 4.16+ with ODF operator available
  2. Storage Nodes: At least 3 nodes with 100GB+ available storage
  3. Cluster Admin Access: Required for ODF installation
  4. Helm 3.x: For deploying charts

docs.title: Step 1: Verify ODF Installation
docs.text:
Check if ODF is already installed:

```bash

docs.title: Check for ODF operator
docs.text: oc get operators | grep odf

docs.title: Check for storage cluster
docs.text: oc get storagecluster -n openshift-storage

docs.title: Check for NooBaa
docs.text:
oc get noobaa -n openshift-storage ```

If ODF is not installed, install it via OperatorHub:

```bash

docs.title: Create openshift-storage namespace
docs.text: oc create namespace openshift-storage

docs.title: Install ODF operator (via OperatorHub UI or CLI)
The operator will automatically create storage cluster
Step 2: Deploy Object Store Infrastructure
docs.text:
Apply the object store manifests:

```bash

docs.title: Deploy NooBaa and ObjectBucketClaims
docs.text: oc apply -f k8s/base/object-store.yaml

docs.title: Verify deployment
docs.text:
oc get noobaa -n openshift-storage oc get objectbucketclaim -n self-healing-platform ```

Wait for ObjectBucketClaims to be bound:

```bash

docs.title: Watch status
docs.text: oc get objectbucketclaim -n self-healing-platform -w

docs.title: Step 3: Extract S3 Credentials
docs.text:
Once buckets are created, extract credentials:

```bash

docs.title: Get S3 endpoint
docs.text: S3_ENDPOINT=$(oc get route s3 -n openshift-storage -o jsonpath='{.spec.host}') echo "S3 Endpoint: https://$S3_ENDPOINT"

docs.title: Get access key
docs.text: ACCESS_KEY=$(oc get secret model-storage -n self-healing-platform -o jsonpath='{.data.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}' | base64 -d) echo "Access Key: $ACCESS_KEY"

docs.title: Get secret key
docs.text: SECRET_KEY=$(oc get secret model-storage -n self-healing-platform -o jsonpath='{.data.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}' | base64 -d) echo "Secret Key: $SECRET_KEY"

docs.title: Get bucket name
docs.text: BUCKET_NAME=$(oc get objectbucketclaim model-storage -n self-healing-platform -o jsonpath='{.spec.generateBucketName}') echo "Bucket Name: $BUCKET_NAME" ```

docs.title: Step 4: Configure Secrets
docs.text:
Update values-secret.yaml.template:

yaml objectStore: endpoint: "https://s3.openshift-storage.svc.cluster.local" accessKey: "<ACCESS_KEY_FROM_STEP_3>" secretKey: "<SECRET_KEY_FROM_STEP_3>" buckets: models: "model-storage" trainingData: "training-data" inferenceResults: "inference-results" region: "us-east-1" sslVerify: false

Copy to secure location:

```bash

docs.title: Create config directory
docs.text: mkdir -p ~/.config/validatedpatterns

docs.title: Copy template
docs.text: cp values-secret.yaml.template ~/.config/validatedpatterns/values-secret-openshift-aiops-platform.yaml

docs.title: Edit with actual values
docs.text: vi ~/.config/validatedpatterns/values-secret-openshift-aiops-platform.yaml

docs.title: Encrypt with ansible-vault (optional but recommended)
docs.text: ansible-vault encrypt ~/.config/validatedpatterns/values-secret-openshift-aiops-platform.yaml ```

docs.title: Step 5: Deploy with Helm
docs.text:
Deploy the platform with object store configuration:

```bash

docs.title: Using make (Validated Patterns way)
docs.text: make install

docs.title: Or using helm directly
docs.text: helm install self-healing-platform ./charts/hub \ -f values-global.yaml \ -f values-hub.yaml \ -f ~/.config/validatedpatterns/values-secret-openshift-aiops-platform.yaml \ -n self-healing-platform \ --create-namespace ```

docs.title: Step 6: Verify Model Serving
docs.text:
Test KServe access to S3:

```bash

docs.title: Check InferenceService
docs.text: oc get inferenceservice -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Check model serving pod logs
docs.text: oc logs -n self-healing-platform -l app=anomaly-detector -f

docs.title: Test S3 access
docs.text: aws s3 ls s3://model-storage/ \ --endpoint-url https://s3.openshift-storage.svc.cluster.local \ --access-key $ACCESS_KEY \ --secret-key $SECRET_KEY ```

docs.title: ObjectBucketClaim stuck in Pending
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check ODF status
docs.text: oc get storagecluster -n openshift-storage

docs.title: Check NooBaa status
docs.text: oc describe noobaa -n openshift-storage

docs.title: Check events
docs.text: oc describe objectbucketclaim model-storage -n self-healing-platform ```

docs.title: KServe can't access S3
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Verify secret exists
docs.text: oc get secret model-storage-config -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Check InferenceService events
docs.text: oc describe inferenceservice anomaly-detector -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Check model serving pod logs
docs.text: oc logs -n self-healing-platform -l app=anomaly-detector --tail=50 ```

docs.title: S3 endpoint not accessible
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Verify NooBaa S3 service
docs.text: oc get svc -n openshift-storage | grep s3

docs.title: Test connectivity from pod
docs.text: oc run -it --rm debug --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- \ curl -k https://s3.openshift-storage.svc.cluster.local ```

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• ADR-020: OpenShift Object Store for Model Serving
  • ADR-010: OpenShift Data Foundation Requirement
  • Validated Patterns: Medical Diagnosis
  • OpenShift Data Foundation Documentation
  • NooBaa Documentation

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: When deploying the openshift-aiops-platform pattern using the Validated Patterns Operator, the operator pod repeatedly crashes with Exit Code 137 (SIGKILL) during large repository clones.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Pattern operator pod shows multiple restarts (10+ restarts)
  • Logs show successful git clone progress but pod terminates before completion
  • Error message: Liveness probe failed: context deadline exceeded
  • Pattern CR status stuck at "Updated status with start event sent"

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
1. Insufficient Memory: The default 512Mi memory limit is too low for cloning large repositories (34,000+ objects)
  2. Aggressive Health Probes: Default liveness probe timeout (15s initial delay, 1s timeout, 20s period) kills the pod during long git clone operations

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: Patch the Pattern operator deployment to increase resources and extend health probe timeouts.

docs.title: Step 1: Apply Resource Patch
docs.text: bash oc patch deployment patterns-operator-controller-manager \ -n openshift-operators \ --type='json' \ -p='[ {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/limits/memory", "value":"2Gi"}, {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/requests/memory", "value":"256Mi"} ]'

docs.title: Step 2: Apply Health Probe Patch
docs.text: bash oc patch deployment patterns-operator-controller-manager \ -n openshift-operators \ --type='json' \ -p='[ {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/livenessProbe/initialDelaySeconds", "value":60}, {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/livenessProbe/timeoutSeconds", "value":10}, {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/livenessProbe/periodSeconds", "value":60}, {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/readinessProbe/initialDelaySeconds", "value":30}, {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/readinessProbe/timeoutSeconds", "value":10}, {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/readinessProbe/periodSeconds", "value":30} ]'

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Deployment
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check pod is running without restarts
docs.text: oc get pods -n openshift-operators | grep pattern

docs.title: Check Pattern operator logs for successful clone
docs.text: oc logs -n openshift-operators deployment/patterns-operator-controller-manager --tail=50

docs.title: Expected output: "Reconcile step 'create application' complete" and "Pattern completed"
Step 4: Verify Pattern Deployment
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check Pattern CR status
docs.text: oc get pattern -n openshift-operators

docs.title: Check ArgoCD application
docs.text: oc get applications.argoproj.io -n openshift-gitops

docs.title: Permanent Fix
docs.text:
To make this fix permanent for new deployments, apply the patch configuration:

bash oc patch deployment patterns-operator-controller-manager \ -n openshift-operators \ --patch-file k8s/operator-patches/patterns-operator-resources.yaml

docs.title: Alternative: Apply During Operator Installation
docs.text: If installing the operator for the first time, modify the operator subscription or deployment YAML to include these resource limits before deployment.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: Tested on: - OpenShift Version: 4.18.21 - Pattern Size: 34,117 git objects, 19,583 compressed - Pattern Operator Version: v0.0.63 - Date: 2025-12-04

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Related Issue: Large repository clones timeout during Validated Patterns deployment
  • Pattern Repository: https://gitea-with-admin-gitea.apps.cluster-fn2qb.fn2qb.sandbox1343.opentlc.com/opentlc-mgr/openshift-aiops-platform.git
  • Deployment Plan: docs/IMPLEMENTATION-PLAN.md

docs.title: Impact
docs.text: After applying this fix: - ✅ Pattern operator pod stable (0 restarts) - ✅ Large repository clones complete successfully - ✅ Pattern CR completes reconciliation - ✅ ArgoCD application created and synced - ✅ Deployment proceeds normally

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This fix is specific to large patterns (30,000+ git objects)
  • Smaller patterns may not require these increased limits
  • Consider the tradeoff between resource usage and deployment reliability
  • Monitor memory usage to adjust limits as needed

docs.title: Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator Upgrade & Volume Support Implementation Plan
docs.text: Date: 2025-12-01 Status: Planning Phase Goal: Upgrade to latest Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator version with enhanced volume support and implement PVC-based model storage workflow

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text: This plan outlines the upgrade path for the Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator to leverage the latest volume support capabilities documented at https://operatorhub.io/operator/jupyter-notebook-validator-operator. The upgrade enables persistent model storage via PVCs, allowing trained models to be shared between notebooks and KServe InferenceServices without S3 dependencies.

docs.title: Key Objectives
docs.text:
1. ✅ Upgrade Operator - Deploy latest version from OperatorHub.io with volume support
  2. ✅ Update Documentation - Align implementation plan with operator-deploy workflow
  3. ✅ Verify Infrastructure - Confirm PVC mounts are correctly configured
  4. ✅ Migrate Notebooks - Update 40+ notebooks to use PVC storage
  5. ✅ Test Workflow - Validate end-to-end model training → serving pipeline

docs.title: ✅ Infrastructure Already Deployed
docs.text: Workbench PVC Mount (CONFIRMED): ```yaml

docs.title: charts/hub/templates/ai-ml-workbench.yaml (lines 154-155, 243-245)
docs.text:
volumeMounts: - name: model-storage mountPath: /mnt/models

volumes: - name: model-storage persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: model-storage-pvc ```

Storage Infrastructure: - ✅ model-storage-pvc (10Gi RWX) - Defined in charts/hub/templates/storage.yaml - ✅ sklearn-pvc-runtime - KServe runtime with PVC support - ✅ model_storage_helpers.py - 380-line helper module with PVC functions

Operator Deployment: - Current Version: v1.0.2 (from ansible/roles/validated_patterns_notebooks/tasks/deploy_operator.yml:187) - Latest Version: v1.0.4-ocp4.20 (Released: 2025-11-30) ✅ UPGRADE AVAILABLE - Installation Method: OLM via OperatorHub.io - Namespace: openshift-operators (AllNamespaces mode) - Channel: alpha - Version Research: See docs/OPERATOR-VERSION-RESEARCH-SUMMARY.md for detailed analysis

docs.title: ❌ Gaps Identified
docs.text:
1. Operator Version: Need to verify latest version on OperatorHub.io
  2. Notebook Storage: 40+ notebooks use local storage (/opt/app-root/src/models) instead of PVC (/mnt/models)
  3. Documentation: Implementation plan doesn't reflect volume support capabilities
  4. Testing: No end-to-end validation of model training → PVC → KServe workflow

docs.title: Task 1.1: Research Latest Operator Version
docs.text:
Action: Query OperatorHub.io for latest Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator version

Current Configuration: ```yaml

docs.title: ansible/roles/validated_patterns_notebooks/defaults/main.yml
docs.text:
notebooks_operator_channel: "alpha" notebooks_operator_catalog_source: "operatorhubio-catalog" notebooks_operatorhubio_catalog_image: "quay.io/operatorhubio/catalog:latest" ```

Research Questions: - What is the latest stable version? - What new volume support features are available? - Are there breaking changes from v1.0.2? - What is the recommended upgrade path?

Deliverable: Version comparison matrix and upgrade decision

docs.title: Task 1.2: Update Ansible Role Configuration
docs.text:
Files to Update: 1. ansible/roles/validated_patterns_notebooks/defaults/main.yml - Update operator version/channel if needed - Add volume support configuration variables

  1. ansible/roles/validated_patterns_notebooks/tasks/deploy_operator.yml
  2. Update version reference (line 187)
  3. Add volume support validation checks

Example Configuration Addition: ```yaml

docs.title: Volume support configuration (NEW)
docs.text: notebooks_enable_volume_support: true notebooks_default_pvc_mount_path: "/mnt/models" notebooks_default_pvc_name: "model-storage-pvc" ```

docs.title: Task 1.3: Test Operator Upgrade
docs.text: Upgrade Workflow: ```bash

docs.title: Step 1: Backup current operator configuration
docs.text: oc get subscription jupyter-notebook-validator-operator -n openshift-operators -o yaml > backup-subscription.yaml oc get csv -n openshift-operators | grep jupyter > backup-csv.txt

docs.title: Step 2: Run Ansible upgrade playbook
docs.text: ansible-playbook ansible/playbooks/operator_deploy_prereqs.yml \ -e notebooks_operator_enabled=true \ -e notebooks_upgrade_operator=true

docs.title: Step 3: Verify upgrade
docs.text:
oc get csv -n openshift-operators | grep jupyter oc get deployment notebook-validator-controller-manager -n openshift-operators ```

Validation Checks: - ✅ Operator deployment ready (1/1 replicas) - ✅ NotebookValidationJob CRD updated - ✅ Webhook configuration valid - ✅ Volume support capabilities available

docs.title: Task 2.1: Update Implementation Plan
docs.text:
File: docs/IMPLEMENTATION-PLAN.md

Changes Required:

  1. Update ADR-029 Status (Line 48) ```markdown # BEFORE: | ADR-029 | Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator | ✅ Proposed, 🔴 Implementation Pending |

# AFTER: | ADR-029 | Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator | ✅ IMPLEMENTED (with PVC volume support) | ```

  1. Replace end2end-deployment with operator-deploy (Lines 397, 444-492, 613) ```markdown # BEFORE: make end2end-deployment

# AFTER: make operator-deploy # Validated Patterns Framework (ADR-019) ```

  1. Deprecate ADR-008 (Line 35) ```markdown # BEFORE: | ADR-008 | Kubeflow Pipelines for MLOps | ✅ Available |

# AFTER: | ADR-008 | Kubeflow Pipelines for MLOps | ⚠️ DEPRECATED | Superseded by ADR-021 (Tekton) + ADR-029 (Notebooks) | ```

  1. Add Model Storage Workflow Section (After Line 546)
  2. Document PVC-based storage architecture
  3. Add workflow diagram: Training → PVC → KServe
  4. Cross-reference ADR-024, ADR-025, ADR-029, ADR-035
  5. Document model_storage_helpers.py API

docs.title: Task 2.2: Update ADR-029
docs.text:
File: docs/adrs/029-jupyter-notebook-validator-operator.md

Add Volume Support Examples: ```yaml

docs.title: NotebookValidationJob with PVC mount
docs.text: apiVersion: mlops.mlops.dev/v1alpha1 kind: NotebookValidationJob metadata: name: model-training-with-pvc spec: notebookPath: notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb volumes: - name: model-storage persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: model-storage-pvc volumeMounts: - name: model-storage mountPath: /mnt/models ```

docs.title: Task 3.1: Update Tier 1 Notebooks (5 notebooks)
docs.text:
Target Notebooks: - 00-setup/environment-setup.ipynb - 00-setup/00-platform-readiness-validation.ipynb - 01-data-collection/openshift-events-analysis.ipynb - 01-data-collection/synthetic-anomaly-generation.ipynb

Migration Pattern: ```python

docs.title: BEFORE (Local Storage):
docs.text: MODELS_DIR = Path('/opt/app-root/src/models') model_path = MODELS_DIR / 'model.pkl' joblib.dump(model, model_path)

docs.title: AFTER (PVC Storage):
docs.text: from model_storage_helpers import save_model_to_pvc, load_model_from_pvc

docs.title: Save to PVC
docs.text:
model_path = save_model_to_pvc( model=model, model_name="anomaly-detector", metadata={'version': '1.0.0', 'accuracy': 0.95} ) print(f"Model saved to PVC: {model_path}") ```

Validation: - ✅ Model saved to /mnt/models/ (PVC) - ✅ Model accessible from other notebooks - ✅ KServe can reference via pvc://model-storage-pvc/model.pkl

docs.title: Task 3.2: Update Tier 2 Notebooks (10 notebooks)
docs.text:
Target Notebooks: All notebooks in 02-anomaly-detection/ and 03-self-healing-logic/

Additional Changes: - Update model loading logic - Add PVC availability checks - Document migration in notebook markdown cells

docs.title: Task 3.3: Update Tier 3 Notebooks (15 notebooks)
docs.text:
Target Notebooks: All notebooks in 04-model-serving/, 05-end-to-end-scenarios/, 06-mcp-lightspeed-integration/, 07-monitoring-operations/, 08-advanced-scenarios/

GPU Notebooks Special Handling: - Ensure PVC mounts work with GPU-enabled pods - Test model training with GPU + PVC storage - Validate KServe deployment with GPU-trained models

docs.title: Test Scenario 1: Model Training → PVC → KServe
docs.text:
Steps: 1. Train model in notebook: 02-anomaly-detection/01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb 2. Save to PVC: save_model_to_pvc(model, "isolation-forest-v1") 3. Deploy to KServe: yaml apiVersion: serving.kserve.io/v1beta1 kind: InferenceService metadata: name: isolation-forest spec: predictor: model: modelFormat: name: sklearn runtime: sklearn-pvc-runtime storageUri: "pvc://model-storage-pvc/isolation-forest-v1.pkl" 4. Test inference: curl -X POST http://isolation-forest.../v1/models/isolation-forest:predict

Success Criteria: - ✅ Model trains successfully - ✅ Model saved to PVC (visible in /mnt/models/) - ✅ KServe InferenceService deploys successfully - ✅ Inference requests return predictions

docs.title: Test Scenario 2: Multi-Notebook Model Sharing
docs.text: Steps: 1. Train model in notebook A 2. Save to PVC with metadata 3. Load model in notebook B 4. Verify model metadata and predictions match

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• ✅ Operator upgraded to latest version
  • ✅ 40+ notebooks migrated to PVC storage
  • ✅ End-to-end workflow validated
  • ✅ Documentation updated and accurate
  • ✅ Zero S3 dependencies for model storage

docs.title: Rollback Plan
docs.text: If upgrade fails: 1. Restore operator subscription: oc apply -f backup-subscription.yaml 2. Revert Ansible configuration changes 3. Notebooks continue using local storage (no breaking changes) 4. Document issues and retry with fixes

docs.title: Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator Upgrade - Test Guide
docs.text: Date: 2025-12-01 Operator Version: v1.0.2 → v1.0.4-ocp4.20 Test Script: scripts/test-operator-upgrade.sh

docs.title: Option 1: Full Test (Recommended)

docs.title: Volume Support Validation
docs.text: After successful operator upgrade, test volume support:

docs.title: Step 1: Apply Test Jobs
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create test NotebookValidationJob with PVC mount

docs.text: ```bash

docs.text: oc exec -n self-healing-platform ${POD_NAME} -- touch /mnt/models/test-file.txt oc exec -n self-healing-platform ${POD_NAME} -- ls -la /mnt/models/test-file.txt ```

docs.title: Step 4: Check Job Logs
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: View job logs

docs.title: Check job status
docs.text: oc get notebookvalidationjob test-volume-support-v1.0.4 -n self-healing-platform -o yaml ```

docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Issue: Execution Environment Image Not Found
docs.text: Solution: ```bash

docs.title: Build execution environment image
docs.text: make build-ee

docs.title: Verify image exists
docs.text: podman images | grep openshift-aiops-platform-ee ```

docs.title: Issue: Operator Not Upgrading
docs.text:
Check subscription: bash oc get subscription jupyter-notebook-validator-operator -n openshift-operators -o yaml

Check install plan: bash oc get installplan -n openshift-operators

Force upgrade: ```bash

docs.title: Delete subscription and rerun
docs.text: oc delete subscription jupyter-notebook-validator-operator -n openshift-operators ./scripts/test-operator-upgrade.sh ```

docs.title: Issue: Pod Not Starting
docs.text:
Check pod events: bash POD_NAME=$(oc get pods -n openshift-operators -l control-plane=controller-manager -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') oc describe pod ${POD_NAME} -n openshift-operators

Check pod logs: bash oc logs ${POD_NAME} -n openshift-operators --tail=100

docs.title: Issue: Volume Mount Fails
docs.text:
Check PVC exists: bash oc get pvc model-storage-pvc -n self-healing-platform

Check PVC status: bash oc describe pvc model-storage-pvc -n self-healing-platform

Check storage class: bash oc get storageclass

docs.title: Rollback Procedure
docs.text:
If issues are encountered after upgrade:

```bash

docs.title: Step 1: Locate backup directory
docs.text: BACKUP_DIR=$(ls -td backups/operator-upgrade-* | head -1) echo "Using backup: ${BACKUP_DIR}"

docs.title: Step 2: Delete current subscription
docs.text: oc delete subscription jupyter-notebook-validator-operator -n openshift-operators

docs.title: Step 3: Restore previous subscription
docs.text: oc apply -f ${BACKUP_DIR}/subscription-before.yaml

docs.title: Step 4: Wait for operator to redeploy
docs.text: oc get csv -n openshift-operators | grep jupyter

docs.title: Step 5: Verify rollback
docs.text: oc get deployment notebook-validator-controller-manager -n openshift-operators ```

docs.title: How to Install OperatorHub.io Operators in OpenShift
docs.text: This guide explains the standard methods for installing operators from OperatorHub.io into OpenShift clusters.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
OperatorHub.io is a community catalog of Kubernetes operators separate from OpenShift's built-in operator catalogs. To use operators from OperatorHub.io in OpenShift, you need to:

  1. Add the OperatorHub.io CatalogSource to your cluster
  2. Install the operator via OLM (Operator Lifecycle Manager)

docs.title: Method 1: OpenShift Web Console (GUI) - Recommended for Most Users
Step 1: Add OperatorHub.io CatalogSource
docs.text:
Option A: Via Web Console 1. Navigate to Administration → Cluster Settings → Configuration → OperatorHub 2. Click Sources tab 3. Click Create CatalogSource 4. Enter: - Name: operatorhubio-catalog - Display Name: OperatorHub.io Community Operators - Publisher: OperatorHub.io - Image: quay.io/operatorhubio/catalog:latest - Source Type: grpc 5. Click Create

Option B: Via CLI bash cat <<EOF | oc apply -f - apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1 kind: CatalogSource metadata: name: operatorhubio-catalog namespace: openshift-marketplace spec: sourceType: grpc image: quay.io/operatorhubio/catalog:latest displayName: OperatorHub.io Community Operators publisher: OperatorHub.io updateStrategy: registryPoll: interval: 60m EOF

docs.title: Step 2: Wait for Catalog to Sync
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check catalog status
docs.text: oc get catalogsource operatorhubio-catalog -n openshift-marketplace

docs.title: Wait for READY status
docs.text: oc get catalogsource operatorhubio-catalog -n openshift-marketplace -o jsonpath='{.status.connectionState.lastObservedState}'

docs.title: Step 3: Install Operator via Web Console
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Operators → OperatorHub
  2. Search for your operator (e.g., "jupyter-notebook-validator-operator")
  3. Click on the operator tile
  4. Click Install
  5. Configure installation:
  6. Update Channel: Select channel (e.g., alpha)
  7. Installation Mode: All namespaces on the cluster (recommended)
  8. Installed Namespace: openshift-operators
  9. Update Approval: Automatic (recommended)
  10. Click Install
  11. Wait for installation to complete (Status: Succeeded)

docs.title: Step 1: Add OperatorHub.io CatalogSource
docs.text: bash oc apply -f - <<EOF apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1 kind: CatalogSource metadata: name: operatorhubio-catalog namespace: openshift-marketplace spec: sourceType: grpc image: quay.io/operatorhubio/catalog:latest displayName: OperatorHub.io Community Operators publisher: OperatorHub.io updateStrategy: registryPoll: interval: 60m EOF

docs.title: Step 2: Create OperatorGroup (if not using openshift-operators)
docs.text:
Note: If installing to openshift-operators namespace, skip this step (OperatorGroup already exists).

bash oc apply -f - <<EOF apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1 kind: OperatorGroup metadata: name: my-operatorgroup namespace: openshift-operators spec: {} EOF

docs.title: Step 3: Create Subscription
docs.text: bash oc apply -f - <<EOF apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1 kind: Subscription metadata: name: jupyter-notebook-validator-operator namespace: openshift-operators spec: channel: alpha name: jupyter-notebook-validator-operator source: operatorhubio-catalog sourceNamespace: openshift-marketplace installPlanApproval: Automatic EOF

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Installation
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check subscription status
docs.text: oc get subscription jupyter-notebook-validator-operator -n openshift-operators

docs.title: Check CSV (ClusterServiceVersion)
docs.text: oc get csv -n openshift-operators | grep jupyter

docs.title: Check operator deployment
docs.text: oc get deployment -n openshift-operators | grep notebook

docs.title: Check operator pods
docs.text: oc get pods -n openshift-operators | grep notebook ```

docs.title: Method 3: Using the OperatorHub.io Install YAML
docs.text:
Each operator on OperatorHub.io provides an install YAML at: https://operatorhub.io/install/<operator-name>.yaml

Example for Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator:

```bash

docs.title: Download the install YAML
docs.text: curl -sL https://operatorhub.io/install/jupyter-notebook-validator-operator.yaml -o /tmp/operator-install.yaml

docs.title: Review the YAML
docs.text: cat /tmp/operator-install.yaml

docs.title: Modify namespace if needed (default is 'operators', change to 'openshift-operators')
docs.text: sed -i 's/namespace: operators/namespace: openshift-operators/g' /tmp/operator-install.yaml

docs.title: Apply the subscription
docs.text:
oc apply -f /tmp/operator-install.yaml ```

⚠️ Important Notes: - The OperatorHub.io YAML typically uses namespace: operators - you may need to change this to openshift-operators for OpenShift - The YAML only creates a Subscription - you still need to add the CatalogSource first (see Method 1 or 2)

docs.title: Issue 1: Operator Not Found in OperatorHub
docs.text: Symptoms: ```bash oc get packagemanifest -n openshift-marketplace

docs.title:
Error: packagemanifests.packages.operators.coreos.com "" not found

```

Solution: 1. Verify CatalogSource exists and is READY: bash oc get catalogsource operatorhubio-catalog -n openshift-marketplace oc get catalogsource operatorhubio-catalog -n openshift-marketplace -o yaml | grep -A 5 "status:"

  1. Wait for catalog to sync (can take 5-10 minutes): bash oc get pods -n openshift-marketplace | grep operatorhubio

  2. Check catalog pod logs: bash oc logs -n openshift-marketplace -l olm.catalogSource=operatorhubio-catalog
Issue 2: CSV Not Created After Subscription
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Subscription exists but no CSV appears - oc get csv -n openshift-operators shows no operator

Solution: 1. Check InstallPlan: bash oc get installplan -n openshift-operators oc describe installplan <installplan-name> -n openshift-operators

  1. Check subscription status: bash oc describe subscription <operator-name> -n openshift-operators

  2. Check for approval requirement: bash oc get installplan -n openshift-operators -o yaml | grep -A 5 "approval:"

  3. Manually approve if needed: bash oc patch installplan <installplan-name> -n openshift-operators --type merge -p '{"spec":{"approved":true}}'

docs.title: Issue 3: Operator Pod Not Starting
docs.text:
Symptoms: - CSV shows "Installing" or "Failed" - Operator deployment exists but pods are not running

Solution: 1. Check deployment status: bash oc get deployment -n openshift-operators oc describe deployment <operator-deployment> -n openshift-operators

  1. Check pod status: bash oc get pods -n openshift-operators oc describe pod <operator-pod> -n openshift-operators

  2. Check pod logs: bash oc logs <operator-pod> -n openshift-operators

  3. Check for image pull errors or resource constraints

docs.title: 1. Use Automatic Approval for Development
docs.text: yaml spec: installPlanApproval: Automatic

docs.title: 2. Use Manual Approval for Production
docs.text: yaml spec: installPlanApproval: Manual

docs.title: 3. Pin to Specific Version (Optional)
docs.text: yaml spec: startingCSV: jupyter-notebook-validator-operator.v1.0.4

docs.title: 4. Monitor Operator Health
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title:
Check CSV status

oc get csv -n openshift-operators -w

Check operator logs

oc logs -f deployment/ -n openshift-operators ```
5. Use Labels for Organization
docs.text: yaml metadata: labels: app.kubernetes.io/name: jupyter-notebook-validator-operator app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: olm

docs.title: Comparison: OpenShift Built-in vs OperatorHub.io
docs.text: Feature OpenShift Built-in OperatorHub.io Catalog Pre-configured Requires CatalogSource Support Red Hat supported Community supported Updates Automatic Manual catalog updates Availability Always available Requires internet access Operators Curated by Red Hat Community contributed

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• OperatorHub.io
  • OpenShift Operator Documentation
  • Operator Lifecycle Manager (OLM)
  • Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator
  • GitHub: jupyter-notebook-validator-operator

docs.title: Red Hat Advanced Cluster Management (ACM) Integration Guide
docs.text: Version: 1.0 Date: 2025-10-31 Status: PRODUCTION READY Related ADR: ADR-022 - Multi-Cluster Support via Red Hat ACM

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Prerequisites
  3. Hub Cluster Installation
  4. Spoke Cluster Registration
  5. GitOps Integration
  6. Policy Management
  7. Monitoring & Observability
  8. Troubleshooting
  9. Best Practices

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Red Hat Advanced Cluster Management (ACM) provides enterprise-grade multi-cluster management for OpenShift. This guide covers ACM integration with the Self-Healing Platform for hub-spoke topology deployments.

docs.title: Key Capabilities
docs.text:
• Cluster Lifecycle Management: Register, monitor, and manage multiple clusters
  • Policy Engine: Enforce consistent policies across clusters
  • Application Management: Deploy applications via GitOps (ArgoCD)
  • Observability: Centralized monitoring and logging
  • Security: RBAC, network policies, and compliance management

docs.title: Hub Cluster Requirements
docs.text:
• OpenShift 4.18+ (tested on 4.18.21)
  • 3+ control plane nodes
  • 4+ worker nodes (minimum)
  • 100GB+ available storage
  • Network connectivity to all spoke clusters
  • Operator Hub access (for operator installation)

docs.title: Spoke Cluster Requirements
docs.text:
• OpenShift 4.16+ (compatible with hub version)
  • 1+ control plane node
  • 2+ worker nodes (minimum)
  • 20GB+ available storage
  • Network connectivity to hub cluster (bidirectional)
  • Outbound HTTPS access to hub cluster

docs.title: Required Tools
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: On hub cluster
docs.text: oc version # OpenShift CLI helm version # Helm 3.x kubectl version # Kubernetes CLI jq # JSON processor yq # YAML processor

docs.title: For cluster registration
docs.text: ansible --version # Ansible 2.9+ ```

docs.title: Step 1: Install ACM Operator
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create namespace for ACM
docs.text: oc create namespace open-cluster-management

docs.title: Create OperatorGroup
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1 kind: OperatorGroup metadata: name: open-cluster-management namespace: open-cluster-management spec: targetNamespaces: - open-cluster-management EOF

docs.title: Subscribe to ACM operator
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1 kind: Subscription metadata: name: advanced-cluster-management namespace: open-cluster-management spec: channel: release-2.9 installPlanApproval: Automatic name: advanced-cluster-management source: redhat-operators sourceNamespace: openshift-marketplace EOF

docs.title: Wait for operator to be ready
docs.text: oc wait --for=condition=Installed \ subscription/advanced-cluster-management \ -n open-cluster-management \ --timeout=300s ```

docs.title: Step 2: Create MultiClusterHub
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create MultiClusterHub resource
docs.text:
cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: operator.open-cluster-management.io/v1 kind: MultiClusterHub metadata: name: multiclusterhub namespace: open-cluster-management spec: imagePullSecret: multiclusterhub-operator-pull-secret availabilityConfig: High enableClusterBackup: true enableAnalytics: true

# Resource requirements nodeSelector: node-role.kubernetes.io/worker: ""

# Tolerations for worker nodes tolerations: - key: workload operator: Equal value: management effect: NoSchedule EOF

docs.title: Wait for MultiClusterHub to be ready
docs.text: oc wait --for=condition=Complete \ multiclusterhub/multiclusterhub \ -n open-cluster-management \ --timeout=600s ```

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Hub Installation
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check ACM operator status
docs.text: oc get csv -n open-cluster-management

docs.title: Check MultiClusterHub status
docs.text: oc get multiclusterhub -n open-cluster-management -o wide

docs.title: Check ACM pods
docs.text: oc get pods -n open-cluster-management | grep -E "hub|manager|console"

docs.title: Access ACM Console
docs.text: ACM_ROUTE=$(oc get route -n open-cluster-management-hub \ multicloud-console -o jsonpath='{.spec.host}') echo "ACM Console: https://$ACM_ROUTE" ```

docs.title: Step 1: Generate Klusterlet Manifests
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: From hub cluster, create cluster import
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: cluster.open-cluster-management.io/v1 kind: ManagedCluster metadata: name: spoke-cluster-1 labels: cloud: openstack region: us-west cluster-type: spoke workload: self-healing spec: hubAcceptsClient: true leaseDurationSeconds: 60 EOF

docs.title: Get klusterlet manifests
docs.text:
oc get secret -n open-cluster-management \ spoke-cluster-1-import \ -o jsonpath='{.data.crds.yaml}' | base64 -d > crds.yaml

oc get secret -n open-cluster-management \ spoke-cluster-1-import \ -o jsonpath='{.data.import.yaml}' | base64 -d > import.yaml ```

docs.title: Step 2: Deploy Klusterlet on Spoke Cluster
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Switch to spoke cluster context
docs.text: oc config use-context spoke-cluster-1

docs.title: Apply CRDs
docs.text: oc apply -f crds.yaml

docs.title: Apply klusterlet manifests
docs.text: oc apply -f import.yaml

docs.title: Wait for klusterlet to be ready
docs.text: oc wait --for=condition=Ready \ managedcluster/spoke-cluster-1 \ -n open-cluster-management \ --timeout=300s ```

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Spoke Registration
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: From hub cluster
docs.text: oc config use-context hub-cluster

docs.title: Check managed cluster status
docs.text: oc get managedcluster spoke-cluster-1 -o wide

docs.title: Check klusterlet status on spoke
docs.text: oc config use-context spoke-cluster-1 oc get pods -n open-cluster-management-agent

docs.title: Verify connectivity
docs.text: oc logs -n open-cluster-management-agent \ -l app=klusterlet-agent \ --tail=50 ```

docs.title: Step 1: Create ApplicationSet for Hub
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create ApplicationSet for hub deployment
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: ApplicationSet metadata: name: self-healing-hub namespace: openshift-gitops spec: generators: - clusters: selector: matchLabels: local-cluster: "true" template: metadata: name: self-healing-hub spec: project: default source: repoURL: https://github.com/your-org/self-healing-platform targetRevision: main path: charts/hub helm: values: | global: environment: hub cluster: local-cluster destination: server: https://kubernetes.default.svc namespace: self-healing-platform syncPolicy: automated: prune: true selfHeal: true EOF ```

docs.title: Step 2: Create ApplicationSet for Spokes
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create ApplicationSet for spoke deployments
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: ApplicationSet metadata: name: self-healing-spokes namespace: openshift-gitops spec: generators: - clusters: selector: matchLabels: cluster-type: spoke template: metadata: name: self-healing-{{name}} spec: project: default source: repoURL: https://github.com/your-org/self-healing-platform targetRevision: main path: charts/spoke helm: values: | global: environment: spoke cluster: {{name}} hubCluster: hub-cluster destination: server: '{{server}}' namespace: self-healing-platform syncPolicy: automated: prune: true selfHeal: true EOF ```

docs.title: Step 1: Create Cluster Policy
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create policy for cluster configuration
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: policy.open-cluster-management.io/v1 kind: Policy metadata: name: self-healing-platform-policy namespace: open-cluster-management-policies spec: remediationAction: enforce disabled: false policy-templates: - objectDefinition: apiVersion: policy.open-cluster-management.io/v1 kind: ConfigurationPolicy metadata: name: self-healing-namespace spec: remediationAction: enforce severity: high object-templates: - complianceType: musthave objectDefinition: apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: self-healing-platform labels: workload: self-healing EOF ```

docs.title: Step 2: Bind Policy to Clusters
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create PlacementBinding
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: policy.open-cluster-management.io/v1 kind: PlacementBinding metadata: name: self-healing-platform-binding namespace: open-cluster-management-policies placementRef: name: self-healing-clusters kind: Placement apiGroup: cluster.open-cluster-management.io subjects: - name: self-healing-platform-policy kind: Policy apiGroup: policy.open-cluster-management.io EOF

docs.title: Create Placement for spoke clusters
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: cluster.open-cluster-management.io/v1beta1 kind: Placement metadata: name: self-healing-clusters namespace: open-cluster-management-policies spec: predicates: - requiredClusterSelector: labelSelector: matchLabels: cluster-type: spoke EOF ```

docs.title: Step 1: Enable Metrics Collection
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Enable observability addon on hub
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: observability.open-cluster-management.io/v1beta2 kind: MultiClusterObservability metadata: name: observability namespace: open-cluster-management-observability spec: enableDownsampling: true enableMetrics: true observabilityAddonSpec: interval: 300 metrics: interval: 300 storageConfig: metricObjectStorage: name: thanos-object-storage key: thanos.yaml EOF ```

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Grafana Dashboards
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create ConfigMap with Grafana dashboard
docs.text: cat << 'EOF' | oc apply -f - apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: self-healing-dashboard namespace: open-cluster-management-observability data: dashboard.json: | { "dashboard": { "title": "Self-Healing Platform - Multi-Cluster", "panels": [ { "title": "Cluster Status", "targets": [ { "expr": "count(up{job=\"kubernetes-apiservers\"}) by (cluster)" } ] } ] } } EOF ```

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Issue: Klusterlet fails to connect to hub

```bash

docs.title: Check klusterlet logs
docs.text: oc logs -n open-cluster-management-agent \ -l app=klusterlet-agent \ --tail=100

docs.title: Verify network connectivity
docs.text:
oc exec -n open-cluster-management-agent \ -it $(oc get pod -n open-cluster-management-agent \ -l app=klusterlet-agent -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') \ -- curl -v https://hub-cluster-api:6443 ```

Issue: ApplicationSet not propagating to spokes

```bash

docs.title: Check ApplicationSet status
docs.text: oc get applicationset -n openshift-gitops -o wide

docs.title: Check ArgoCD Application status
docs.text: oc get application -n openshift-gitops -o wide

docs.title: Check cluster selector
docs.text: oc get managedcluster -L cluster-type ```

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Cluster Labeling: Use consistent labels for cluster selection
  2. Policy Enforcement: Start with audit mode before enforcement
  3. Network Security: Use network policies to restrict cluster communication
  4. Resource Quotas: Set resource quotas on spoke clusters
  5. Backup Strategy: Enable cluster backup for disaster recovery
  6. Monitoring: Monitor ACM components and cluster health
  7. Documentation: Maintain runbooks for common operations
  8. Testing: Test policies and applications in staging first

For more information: - Red Hat ACM Documentation - ADR-022: Multi-Cluster Support - Validated Patterns Framework

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This guide explains how to properly manage secrets and credentials in the OpenShift AI Ops Platform without committing them to git.

⚠️ CRITICAL: This project previously had secrets leaked to the public GitHub repository. This guide ensures that never happens again.

docs.title: Security Principles
docs.text:
1. NEVER commit secrets to git - Use environment variables or External Secrets Operator
  2. Use .gitignore - Ensure sensitive files are git-ignored
  3. Rotate credentials regularly - Change secrets every 90 days minimum
  4. Use External Secrets Operator - Store secrets in external vaults (AWS, HashiCorp, Azure)
  5. Implement pre-commit hooks - Scan for secrets before commits

docs.title: Get Your Token
docs.text:
1. Visit Red Hat Automation Hub
  2. Log in with your Red Hat account
  3. Click "Load Token"
  4. Copy the token (starts with eyJhbGc...)

docs.title: Option A: Environment Variable (Recommended for CI/CD)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Export token
docs.text: export ANSIBLE_HUB_TOKEN='eyJhbGc...'

docs.title: Or add to ~/.bashrc
docs.text: echo "export ANSIBLE_HUB_TOKEN='eyJhbGc...'" >> ~/.bashrc source ~/.bashrc

docs.title: Option B: Local Configuration File (Recommended for Development)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Copy example
docs.text: cp ansible.cfg.example ansible.cfg.local

docs.title: Edit ansible.cfg.local and replace YOUR_TOKEN_HERE with real token
docs.text: vi ansible.cfg.local

docs.title: Use local config
docs.text: export ANSIBLE_CONFIG=ansible.cfg.local

docs.title: Verify
docs.text: ansible-galaxy collection list ```

docs.title: Revoke Compromised Token
docs.text:
If your token is leaked:

  1. Visit Red Hat Automation Hub
  2. Click "Load Token"
  3. Click "Revoke" button (⚠️ This invalidates the current token)
  4. Generate new token
  5. Update your environment variable or local config file

docs.title: Get NooBaa Credentials
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Get access key
docs.text: oc get secret noobaa-admin -n openshift-storage \ -o jsonpath='{.data.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}' | base64 -d

docs.title: Get secret key
docs.text: oc get secret noobaa-admin -n openshift-storage \ -o jsonpath='{.data.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}' | base64 -d ```

docs.title: Option A: External Secrets Operator (RECOMMENDED)
docs.text:
Create ExternalSecret:

```yaml

docs.title: k8s/base/s3-credentials-externalsecret.yaml
docs.text:
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1 kind: ExternalSecret metadata: name: s3-credentials namespace: self-healing-platform spec: refreshInterval: 1h secretStoreRef: name: aws-secretstore kind: SecretStore target: name: s3-credentials creationPolicy: Owner data: - secretKey: accessKey remoteRef: key: self-healing-platform/s3 property: access_key_id - secretKey: secretKey remoteRef: key: self-healing-platform/s3 property: secret_access_key ```

Deploy:

bash oc apply -f k8s/base/s3-credentials-externalsecret.yaml

docs.title: Option B: values-secret.yaml (Development)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Copy example
docs.text: cp charts/hub/values-secret.yaml.example charts/hub/values-secret.yaml

docs.title: Edit and add real credentials
docs.text: vi charts/hub/values-secret.yaml

docs.title: Deploy (Helm will merge values-secret.yaml with values-global.yaml)
docs.text: make -f common/Makefile operator-deploy ```

docs.title: Rotate NooBaa Credentials
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Delete old credentials
docs.text: oc exec -n openshift-storage \ $(oc get pods -n openshift-storage -l app=noobaa-core -o name) \ -- noobaa-cli account delete noobaa-admin

docs.title: Create new credentials
docs.text: oc exec -n openshift-storage \ $(oc get pods -n openshift-storage -l app=noobaa-core -o name) \ -- noobaa-cli account create noobaa-admin --new_password

docs.title: Generate Webhook Secret
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Generate random secret
docs.text: openssl rand -hex 20

docs.title: Create Kubernetes secret
docs.text: oc create secret generic github-webhook-secret \ --from-literal=secretToken=$(openssl rand -hex 20) \ -n openshift-pipelines

docs.title: Get webhook URL
docs.text: oc get route el-github-webhook-listener -n openshift-pipelines -o jsonpath='{.spec.host}' ```

docs.title: Configure GitHub Webhook
docs.text:
1. Go to repository Settings → Webhooks → Add webhook
  2. Payload URL: https://<route-from-above>
  3. Content type: application/json
  4. Secret: Paste the generated secret token
  5. Events: Select "Just the push event"
  6. Click "Add webhook"

docs.title: Configure Gitea Webhook
docs.text:
1. Go to repository Settings → Webhooks → Add webhook → Gitea
  2. Target URL: https://<route-from-above>
  3. HTTP Method: POST
  4. POST Content Type: application/json
  5. Secret: Paste the generated secret token
  6. Trigger On: Push events
  7. Click "Add webhook"

docs.title: 4. Pre-Commit Secret Scanning
docs.text: Install and configure pre-commit hooks to prevent secret commits:

docs.title: Install detect-secrets
docs.text: bash pip install detect-secrets

docs.title: Create .pre-commit-config.yaml
docs.text: yaml repos: - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets rev: v1.4.0 hooks: - id: detect-secrets args: ['--baseline', '.secrets.baseline'] exclude: | (?x)^( .*\.example$| .*\.md$| SECURITY-INCIDENT-RESPONSE\.md )

docs.title: Setup Baseline
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Generate baseline (scan current repo)
docs.text: detect-secrets scan --baseline .secrets.baseline

docs.title: Install pre-commit hooks
docs.text: pre-commit install

docs.title: Test
docs.text: pre-commit run --all-files ```

docs.title: Update Baseline (After Remediation)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: After fixing secrets, update baseline
docs.text: detect-secrets scan --baseline .secrets.baseline --force ```

docs.title: 5. Automated Secret Scanning in CI/CD
docs.text: Add to Tekton pipeline or GitHub Actions:

docs.title: Tekton Task
docs.text: yaml apiVersion: tekton.dev/v1beta1 kind: Task metadata: name: secret-scan spec: steps: - name: detect-secrets image: python:3.9-slim script: | #!/bin/bash pip install detect-secrets detect-secrets scan --baseline .secrets.baseline if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then echo "❌ SECRETS DETECTED IN CODE!" exit 1 fi

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
```yaml name: Secret Scanning on: [push, pull_request]

jobs: secret-scan: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Install detect-secrets run: pip install detect-secrets - name: Scan for secrets run: detect-secrets scan --baseline .secrets.baseline ```

docs.title: 6. Credential Rotation Schedule
docs.text: Credential Type Rotation Frequency Owner Notes Red Hat OAuth Token 180 days Platform Team Expires automatically S3/NooBaa Credentials 90 days Platform Team Rotate via noobaa-cli Webhook Secrets 90 days DevOps Team Update GitHub/Gitea webhooks Gitea Admin Password 90 days Platform Team Update via Gitea UI Service Account Tokens N/A Kubernetes Auto-rotated by K8s

docs.title: 7. Security Incident Response
docs.text:
If secrets are accidentally committed:

  1. IMMEDIATELY revoke all exposed credentials
  2. DO NOT just delete the files - they remain in git history
  3. Use git-filter-repo to remove secrets from history: bash chmod +x scripts/remove-secrets-from-git-history.sh ./scripts/remove-secrets-from-git-history.sh
  4. Force push to remote (requires coordination with team)
  5. Notify all contributors to re-clone repository
  6. Update SECURITY-INCIDENT-RESPONSE.md with timeline
  7. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

docs.title: 8. Checklist for New Contributors
docs.text:
Before committing any code:

  • [ ] Read this security guide completely
  • [ ] Install pre-commit hooks: pre-commit install
  • [ ] Never commit ansible.cfg.local, values-secret.yaml, or *.env files
  • [ ] Use example files (.example suffix) for templates
  • [ ] Verify .gitignore includes sensitive files
  • [ ] Test pre-commit hooks: pre-commit run --all-files
  • [ ] Use External Secrets Operator for production deployments

docs.title: 9. Resources
docs.text:
• External Secrets Operator Documentation
  • Red Hat External Secrets Operator for OpenShift
  • ADR-026: Secrets Management Automation
  • detect-secrets Documentation
  • OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
Contact: Platform Security Team

Last Updated: 2025-12-10 Version: 1.0 Status: CRITICAL - MANDATORY READING

docs.title: Local Reference Guide for OpenShift AIOps Platform
docs.text: Date: 2025-11-02 Toolkit Version: 1.0.0 Local Clone: my-pattern/ (git-ignored) Repository: https://github.com/tosin2013/validated-patterns-ansible-toolkit

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The validated-patterns-ansible-toolkit has been cloned locally to my-pattern/ for reference and development. This directory is git-ignored to prevent accidental commits while providing easy access to the toolkit's roles, playbooks, and documentation.

docs.title: Local Reference Structure
docs.text: my-pattern/ ├── ansible/ │ ├── roles/ # 8 production-ready Ansible roles │ │ ├── validated_patterns_prerequisites/ │ │ ├── validated_patterns_common/ │ │ ├── validated_patterns_operator/ │ │ ├── validated_patterns_deploy/ │ │ ├── validated_patterns_gitea/ │ │ ├── validated_patterns_secrets/ │ │ ├── validated_patterns_validate/ │ │ └── validated_patterns_cleanup/ │ └── playbooks/ # Example playbooks │ ├── deploy_complete_pattern.yml │ ├── install_gitops.yml │ ├── test_prerequisites.yml │ └── cleanup_pattern.yml ├── docs/ # Comprehensive documentation │ ├── ANSIBLE-ROLES-REFERENCE.md │ ├── DEVELOPER-GUIDE.md │ ├── END-USER-GUIDE.md │ ├── QUICK-START-ROLES.md │ ├── TROUBLESHOOTING-COMPREHENSIVE.md │ └── adr/ # Architectural Decision Records ├── collection/ # Ansible Collection │ └── tosin2013.validated_patterns_toolkit/ ├── tests/ # Integration tests │ ├── integration/ │ ├── week8/ │ ├── week9/ │ └── week10/ └── ONBOARDING.md # Getting started guide

docs.title: 1. validated_patterns_prerequisites
docs.text:
Purpose: Validate cluster prerequisites before deployment

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_prerequisites/

Key Tasks: - Verify OpenShift cluster version - Check cluster resources (CPU, memory, storage) - Validate operator availability - Test cluster connectivity

Example Usage: yaml - name: Validate cluster prerequisites include_role: name: validated_patterns_prerequisites vars: min_openshift_version: "4.18" required_operators: - openshift-gitops-operator - openshift-pipelines-operator

Related ADRs: ADR-001 (OpenShift Platform Selection)

docs.title: 2. validated_patterns_common
docs.text:
Purpose: Deploy common GitOps infrastructure (Helm, ArgoCD)

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_common/

Key Tasks: - Install Helm - Deploy OpenShift GitOps (ArgoCD) - Configure GitOps repositories - Setup ArgoCD applications

Example Usage: yaml - name: Deploy GitOps infrastructure include_role: name: validated_patterns_common vars: gitops_enabled: true gitops_namespace: openshift-gitops helm_version: "3.12.0"

Related ADRs: ADR-019 (Validated Patterns Framework), ADR-027 (CI/CD Automation)

docs.title: 3. validated_patterns_operator
docs.text:
Purpose: Deploy patterns using the Validated Patterns Operator

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_operator/

Key Tasks: - Install Validated Patterns Operator - Create Pattern custom resources - Configure multi-cluster support - Manage pattern lifecycle

Example Usage: yaml - name: Deploy via Validated Patterns Operator include_role: name: validated_patterns_operator vars: pattern_name: self-healing-platform pattern_repo: https://github.com/KubeHeal/openshift-aiops-platform.git pattern_branch: main

Related ADRs: ADR-020 (Bootstrap Deployment Lifecycle), ADR-029 (Multi-Cluster)

docs.title: 4. validated_patterns_deploy
docs.text:
Purpose: Deploy applications and configure Tekton pipelines

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_deploy/

Key Tasks: - Deploy Tekton pipelines - Configure pipeline triggers - Deploy applications via Helm - Setup CI/CD workflows

Example Usage: yaml - name: Deploy Tekton pipelines include_role: name: validated_patterns_deploy vars: deploy_tekton_pipelines: true tekton_namespace: openshift-pipelines pipelines: - deployment-validation-pipeline - model-serving-validation-pipeline

Related ADRs: ADR-023 (Tekton Configuration), ADR-027 (CI/CD Automation)

docs.title: 5. validated_patterns_gitea
docs.text:
Purpose: Deploy Gitea local Git repository

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_gitea/

Key Tasks: - Deploy Gitea operator - Create Gitea instance - Configure admin and user accounts - Setup repository mirroring

Example Usage: yaml - name: Deploy Gitea include_role: name: validated_patterns_gitea vars: gitea_namespace: gitea gitea_admin_user: opentlc-mgr gitea_ssl_enabled: true gitea_create_users: true

Related ADRs: ADR-028 (Gitea Local Git Repository)

docs.title: 6. validated_patterns_secrets
docs.text:
Purpose: Manage secrets with External Secrets Operator

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_secrets/

Key Tasks: - Deploy External Secrets Operator - Configure SecretStores - Create ExternalSecrets - Integrate with Vault/AWS Secrets Manager

Example Usage: yaml - name: Deploy secrets management include_role: name: validated_patterns_secrets vars: secrets_backend: external-secrets secrets_vault_enabled: true secrets_vault_address: "https://vault.example.com" secrets_rotation_enabled: true

Related ADRs: ADR-024 (External Secrets Model Storage), ADR-026 (Secrets Management Automation)

docs.title: 7. validated_patterns_validate
docs.text:
Purpose: Validate pattern deployment and health

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_validate/

Key Tasks: - Run deployment validation tests - Check application health - Verify GitOps sync status - Generate validation reports

Example Usage: yaml - name: Validate deployment include_role: name: validated_patterns_validate vars: validate_gitops: true validate_tekton: true validate_applications: true

Related ADRs: ADR-021 (Tekton Pipeline Deployment Validation)

docs.title: 8. validated_patterns_cleanup
docs.text:
Purpose: Clean up pattern resources

Location: my-pattern/ansible/roles/validated_patterns_cleanup/

Key Tasks: - Remove pattern applications - Clean up operators - Delete namespaces - Restore cluster state

Example Usage: yaml - name: Cleanup pattern include_role: name: validated_patterns_cleanup vars: cleanup_namespaces: - self-healing-platform - gitea cleanup_operators: true

docs.title: Deploy Complete Pattern
docs.text: Location: my-pattern/ansible/playbooks/deploy_complete_pattern.yml

docs.title: ```yaml
docs.text:
• name: Deploy Complete Validated Pattern hosts: localhost gather_facts: false

tasks: - name: Validate prerequisites include_role: name: validated_patterns_prerequisites

- name: Deploy GitOps infrastructure
  include_role:
    name: validated_patterns_common

- name: Deploy pattern via operator
  include_role:
    name: validated_patterns_operator

- name: Validate deployment
  include_role:
    name: validated_patterns_validate

```

docs.text: Location: my-pattern/ansible/playbooks/install_gitops.yml

docs.title: ```yaml
docs.text:
• name: Install OpenShift GitOps hosts: localhost gather_facts: false

roles: - validated_patterns_common ```

docs.title: ✅ Infrastructure Validation Notebook - Implementation Complete
docs.text: Date: 2025-11-04 Status: ✅ COMPLETE - Ready for deployment and testing ADR: ADR-029 - Infrastructure Validation Notebook for User Readiness

docs.title: 📋 Summary
docs.text: Successfully created a comprehensive platform readiness validation system that can be executed both manually in Jupyter notebooks and automatically via Tekton pipelines.

docs.title: 1. ✅ ADR-029: Architecture Decision Record
docs.text:
File: docs/adrs/029-infrastructure-validation-notebook.md

  • Comprehensive architecture decision for infrastructure validation
  • 35+ validation checks defined across 4 categories
  • Dual execution model (Jupyter + Tekton)
  • JSON and Markdown reporting formats
  • Complete implementation plan with phases

docs.title: 2. ✅ Validation Helpers Module
docs.text:
File: notebooks/utils/validation_helpers.py (799 lines)

Features: - 15+ validation functions implemented - 4 validation categories: - Basic Environment (Python, PyTorch, GPU, Storage) - Platform Infrastructure (Coordination Engine, Model Serving, S3, MCP, Prometheus) - OpenShift Components (ArgoCD, Tekton, External Secrets) - Network Connectivity - HTTP retry logic (3 attempts for reliability) - JSON report generation - Comprehensive error handling and remediation guidance - Optional boto3 import for environments without AWS SDK

Key Functions: ```python

docs.title: Basic Environment
docs.text: validate_python_version() validate_pytorch_installation() validate_gpu_availability() validate_storage_volumes()

docs.title: Platform Infrastructure
docs.text: validate_coordination_engine() validate_coordination_engine_metrics() validate_model_serving() validate_object_storage() validate_mcp_server() validate_prometheus()

docs.title: OpenShift Components
docs.text: validate_argocd_applications() validate_tekton_pipelines() validate_external_secrets()

docs.title: Report Generation
docs.text: generate_validation_report(checks) print_validation_report(report) ```

docs.title: 3. ✅ Validation Notebook
docs.text:
File: notebooks/00-setup/00-platform-readiness-validation.ipynb

Structure: - 8 cells (7 code cells, 1 markdown) - Comprehensive overview and instructions - Step-by-step validation execution - Visual status indicators - Generates JSON and visual reports - Provides actionable next steps

Execution Flow: 1. Import validation functions 2. Validate basic environment (6 checks) 3. Validate platform infrastructure (12 checks) 4. Validate OpenShift components (10 checks) 5. Generate comprehensive report 6. Display results with visual indicators 7. Show summary statistics 8. Provide next steps based on status

docs.title: 4. ✅ Tekton Task
docs.text:
File: tekton/tasks/platform-readiness-validation-task.yaml

Features: - Clones repository - Installs dependencies (papermill, nbconvert) - Executes validation notebook programmatically - Converts output to HTML artifact - Extracts JSON report - Analyzes results and fails on critical issues - Provides detailed failure information

Results Produced: - validation-status: Overall status (PASSED/FAILED/DEGRADED) - checks-passed: Number of passed checks - checks-failed: Number of failed checks - checks-warnings: Number of warnings - report-path: Path to JSON report

docs.title: 5. ✅ Tekton Pipeline
docs.text:
File: tekton/pipelines/platform-readiness-validation-pipeline.yaml

Features: - Orchestrates validation task - Configurable parameters (git-url, git-revision, notebook-path) - Shared workspace for artifacts - Results propagation - Finally block with summary

docs.title: Category 1: Basic Environment (6 checks)
docs.text:
• ✅ Python version (3.11+)
  • ✅ PyTorch installation (2025.1)
  • ✅ GPU availability (warning if not available)
  • ✅ Data volume mounted and writable
  • ✅ Models volume mounted and writable
  • ✅ Config directory writable

docs.title: Category 2: Platform Infrastructure (12 checks)
docs.text:
• ✅ Coordination Engine health endpoint
  • ✅ Coordination Engine metrics endpoint
  • ✅ Model Serving: anomaly-detector InferenceService
  • ✅ Model Serving: predictive-analytics InferenceService
  • ✅ Object Storage: S3 credentials available
  • ✅ Object Storage: S3 connectivity
  • ✅ Object Storage: model-storage bucket exists
  • ✅ Monitoring: Prometheus query API
  • ✅ MCP Server: health endpoint
  • ✅ MCP Server: tool availability

docs.title: Category 3: OpenShift Components (10 checks)
docs.text:
• ✅ ArgoCD: self-healing-platform application
  • ✅ Tekton: deployment-validation-pipeline
  • ✅ Tekton: model-serving-validation-pipeline
  • ✅ External Secrets: SecretStore ready
  • ✅ External Secrets: gitea-credentials synced
  • ✅ External Secrets: registry-credentials synced
  • ✅ External Secrets: database-credentials synced

docs.title: Category 4: Network Connectivity (7 checks)
docs.text:
• ✅ In-cluster DNS resolution
  • ✅ Service-to-service communication
  • ✅ Pod-to-pod networking
  • ✅ API endpoint accessibility

Total: 35+ validation checks

docs.title: JSON Report
docs.text:
Location: /opt/app-root/src/.config/validation-report.json

json { "validation_date": "2025-11-04T17:30:00Z", "validation_status": "PASSED", "summary": { "total_checks": 35, "passed": 33, "failed": 0, "warnings": 2 }, "checks": [...] }

docs.title: Visual Report
docs.text:
• Status indicators (✅ ⚠️ ❌)
  • Category grouping
  • Response times for HTTP checks
  • Remediation guidance for failures
  • Summary table with percentages

docs.title: Option 1: Manual Execution (Jupyter Notebook)
docs.text:
1. Open RHODS Workbench
  2. Navigate to notebook: notebooks/00-setup/00-platform-readiness-validation.ipynb
  3. Run all cells: Click "Run All" or execute cells sequentially
  4. Review results: Check validation status and any failures/warnings
  5. Apply remediations: Follow guidance for any failed checks
  6. Re-run if needed: After applying fixes, re-execute notebook

docs.title: Option 2: Automated Execution (Tekton Pipeline)
docs.text: Deploy Tekton resources: ```bash

docs.title: Deploy task
docs.text: oc apply -f tekton/tasks/platform-readiness-validation-task.yaml

docs.title: Deploy pipeline
docs.text:
oc apply -f tekton/pipelines/platform-readiness-validation-pipeline.yaml ```

Create PipelineRun: bash tkn pipeline start platform-readiness-validation-pipeline \ --param git-url=https://github.com/KubeHeal/openshift-aiops-platform.git \ --param git-revision=main \ --workspace name=shared-workspace,claimName=validation-workspace-pvc \ --showlog

Check results: bash tkn pipelinerun logs <pipelinerun-name> -f

docs.title: ✅ Testing Results
docs.text:
Syntax Validation: ✅ PASSED - Python syntax valid - Imports working (with optional boto3) - No syntax errors

Expected Behavior: - ✅ In RHODS workbench: All checks will execute - ✅ In Tekton pipeline: Automated execution with papermill - ⚠️ On lab system: Some checks will fail (expected - not in workbench)

docs.title: With Existing Systems
docs.text:
1. Notebooks README: Update to include validation workflow
  2. IMPLEMENTATION-PLAN.md: Add validation milestone
  3. Deployment Workflow: Run validation after platform deployment
  4. CI/CD: Integrate Tekton pipeline in webhook triggers

docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Deploy Platform] --> B[Run Validation Notebook]
    B --> C{Validation Status}
    C -->|PASSED| D[Execute Notebooks 01-08]
    C -->|DEGRADED| E[Review Warnings]
    C -->|FAILED| F[Apply Remediations]
    E --> D
    F --> B

docs.text:
• ✅ Coverage: 35+ infrastructure components validated
  • ✅ Reliability: 3-attempt retry logic for network checks
  • ✅ Performance: <3 minutes execution time (estimated)
  • ✅ Automation: Dual execution (manual + Tekton)
  • ✅ Usability: Clear visual indicators and remediation steps

docs.title: Volume Support Issue - Jupyter Notebook Validator Operator v1.0.4-ocp4.18
docs.text: Date: December 1, 2025 Operator Version: v1.0.4-ocp4.18 Issue Status: Confirmed - Feature Not Implemented

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Volume support (volumes and volumeMounts) is NOT working in v1.0.4-ocp4.18. The operator accepts the configuration in the NotebookValidationJob spec but does not pass it through to the validation pods.

docs.title: Test Configuration
docs.text:
Created a NotebookValidationJob with custom volumes:

yaml apiVersion: mlops.mlops.dev/v1alpha1 kind: NotebookValidationJob metadata: name: test-volume-simple namespace: jupyter-notebook-validator-system spec: podConfig: volumes: - name: test-data emptyDir: {} - name: test-models emptyDir: {} volumeMounts: - name: test-data mountPath: /mnt/data - name: test-models mountPath: /mnt/models

docs.title: Expected Behavior
docs.text: The validation pod should have the custom volumes mounted at /mnt/data and /mnt/models.

docs.title: Actual Behavior
docs.text:
The validation pod was created with only default volumes: - workspace (emptyDir) - jovyan-home (emptyDir) - kube-api-access-* (service account token)

Custom volumes (test-data, test-models) were NOT present in the pod.

docs.title: Pod Volume Configuration
docs.text: bash $ oc get pod test-volume-simple-validation -n jupyter-notebook-validator-system -o yaml | grep -A 30 "volumes:" volumes: - emptyDir: {} name: workspace - emptyDir: {} name: jovyan-home - name: kube-api-access-mcstc projected: ...

docs.title: Pod Volume Mounts
docs.text:
bash $ oc get pod test-volume-simple-validation -n jupyter-notebook-validator-system -o yaml | grep -A 15 "volumeMounts:" volumeMounts: - mountPath: /workspace name: workspace - mountPath: /home/jovyan name: jovyan-home - mountPath: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount name: kube-api-access-mcstc readOnly: true

No /mnt/data or /mnt/models mounts present.

docs.title: Operator Logs
docs.text: No errors or warnings about volume configuration. The operator logs show normal reconciliation but no mention of processing custom volumes.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: The operator's pod creation logic does not include code to: 1. Read the volumes and volumeMounts fields from spec.podConfig 2. Merge custom volumes with default volumes 3. Add custom volume mounts to the validation pod containers

docs.title: Impact
docs.text: Users cannot use persistent volumes or custom volume mounts with the operator. This affects: - ✗ Mounting PVCs for model storage - ✗ Mounting ConfigMaps for configuration - ✗ Mounting Secrets for credentials - ✗ Using emptyDir for shared data between init containers and main container - ✗ Any use case requiring custom volume mounts

docs.title: Workaround
docs.text: None available. The feature must be implemented in the operator code.

docs.title: Recommendation for Developers
docs.text:
The operator needs to be updated to:

  1. Parse volume configuration from NotebookValidationJob spec: go volumes := nvj.Spec.PodConfig.Volumes volumeMounts := nvj.Spec.PodConfig.VolumeMounts

  2. Merge with default volumes: go podSpec.Volumes = append(defaultVolumes, volumes...)

  3. Add volume mounts to containers: go for _, container := range podSpec.Containers { container.VolumeMounts = append(container.VolumeMounts, volumeMounts...) }

  4. Validate volume references:

  5. Ensure all volumeMounts reference existing volumes
  6. Check for mount path conflicts
  7. Validate PVC existence if using persistentVolumeClaim

docs.title: Related Files
docs.text:
• Test manifest: tests/test-volume-simple.yaml
  • Operator deployment: manifests/jupyter-operator-direct-deployment.yaml
  • CRD definition: Applied from bundle v1.0.4-ocp4.18

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. ✅ Issue documented for developers
  2. ⏳ Waiting for operator code fix
  3. ⏳ Test with updated operator version
  4. ⏳ Resume implementation plan after fix is deployed

docs.title: Operator Installation Status
docs.text:
The operator itself is fully operational: - ✅ Deployment: 1/1 READY - ✅ Pods: 2/2 Running - ✅ Controller: Started with leader election - ✅ Webhooks: Enabled and serving - ✅ CRD: Installed and functional

Only the volume support feature is not working.

docs.title: CI/CD Webhook Automation - Implementation Complete
docs.text: Date: 2025-11-02 Status: ✅ COMPLETE - Webhook automation fully operational ADR: ADR-027: CI/CD Pipeline Automation

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
The CI/CD webhook automation is fully operational and ready for platform deployment. The EventListener successfully receives webhook events from Gitea, triggers Tekton pipelines, and creates PipelineRuns automatically on every git push to the main branch.

Key Achievement: Automated CI/CD pipeline execution via webhooks, enabling continuous validation of platform deployments.

docs.title: 1. Webhook Secret Generation ✅

docs.title: Correct Deployment Workflow
docs.text: The webhook automation revealed the correct workflow order:

docs.title: Step 1: Deploy Platform Infrastructure (NEXT TASK)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Create namespace
docs.text: oc create namespace self-healing-platform

docs.title: Deploy platform via Validated Patterns framework
docs.text:
make end2end-deployment ```

What Gets Deployed: - Common infrastructure (Helm, ArgoCD) - Secrets management (External Secrets Operator) - Operators (GitOps, Pipelines, AI, KServe, GPU, ODF) - Storage configuration (PVCs, storage classes, S3) - Coordination engine (deployment, service, database) - Monitoring stack (Prometheus, Grafana, alerts)

docs.title: Step 2: Run Tekton Validation Pipeline
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Trigger validation manually
docs.text: tkn pipeline start deployment-validation-pipeline \ -p namespace=self-healing-platform \ -p cluster-version=4.18 \ -n openshift-pipelines

docs.title: Or trigger via webhook (automatic on git push)
docs.text:
git commit -m "trigger validation" git push gitea main ```

What Gets Validated: - Prerequisites (cluster connectivity, version, tools, RBAC) - Operators (GitOps, Pipelines, AI, KServe, GPU, ODF, ESO) - Storage (classes, PVCs, ODF, S3) - Model serving infrastructure (KServe, namespaces, RBAC) - Coordination engine (deployment, health, API, database) - Monitoring (Prometheus, alerts, Grafana, logging)

docs.title: Step 3: Execute Notebooks for Model Training
docs.text:
After validation passes, users execute notebooks:

  1. notebooks/00-setup/environment-setup.ipynb - Environment setup
  2. notebooks/01-data-collection/*.ipynb - Data collection
  3. notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/*.ipynb - Model training
  4. notebooks/03-self-healing-logic/*.ipynb - Remediation logic
  5. notebooks/04-model-serving/kserve-model-deployment.ipynb - Model deployment
  6. notebooks/05-end-to-end-scenarios/complete-platform-demo.ipynb - End-to-end demo

Model Artifacts: - arima_model.pkl - ARIMA time-series model - lstm_autoencoder.pt - LSTM autoencoder (PyTorch) - lstm_scaler.pkl - LSTM data scaler - ensemble_config.pkl - Ensemble configuration

docs.title: Step 4: Final Validation with Model Serving
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Run model-serving-validation-pipeline
docs.text: tkn pipeline start model-serving-validation-pipeline \ -p namespace=self-healing-platform \ -n openshift-pipelines

docs.title: Or run full validation pipeline
docs.text:
tkn pipeline start deployment-validation-pipeline \ -p namespace=self-healing-platform \ -p cluster-version=4.18 \ -n openshift-pipelines ```

What Gets Validated: - InferenceServices are ready - Model endpoints accessible - Model predictions working - End-to-end workflow completes successfully

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
1. Validation Requires Deployed Platform
  2. Validation pipeline checks deployed components
  3. Cannot validate what hasn't been deployed yet

  4. Webhook automation is separate from platform deployment

  5. Correct Workflow Order

  6. Infrastructure deployment first
  7. Validation to verify infrastructure
  8. Notebooks to train and deploy models

  9. Final validation to verify models

  10. Tool Dependencies

  11. Validation tasks require jq, oc, kubectl, helm, yq
  12. maintenance-tools image provides all required tools

  13. Image selection is critical for task success

  14. RBAC Permissions

  15. EventListener requires permissions for interceptors and clusterinterceptors
  16. ClusterRole must include all Tekton Triggers resources
  17. ServiceAccount must be bound to ClusterRole
