  • Framework Repository: https://github.com/validatedpatterns/common
  • Pattern Examples: https://github.com/validatedpatterns/patterns
  • Community Slack: https://hybridcloudpatterns.slack.com

docs.title: Q: Can I submit a pattern that requires GPU?
docs.text: A: Yes, but document GPU as optional for broader adoption. Support both GPU and non-GPU deployments.

docs.title: Q: What tier should I use for initial submission?
docs.text: A: Start with tier: sandbox. Graduate to tested after community validation.

docs.title: Q: How long does the review process take?
docs.text: A: Typically 1-2 weeks, depending on PR complexity and reviewer availability.

docs.title: Q: Can I update the pattern after submission?
docs.text: A: Yes! Submit PRs to update your pattern in the validated-patterns repository.

docs.title: Q: What if my pattern fails validation?
docs.text:
A: Address the feedback and re-submit. The validation script helps catch issues early.

Next Steps:

  1. Run ./scripts/validate-pattern-submission.sh
  2. Test on fresh SNO and HA clusters
  3. Fork https://github.com/validatedpatterns/patterns
  4. Create PR with your pattern
  5. Engage with community feedback

Good luck with your submission! 🚀

docs.title: How to
docs.text: Task-oriented guides for specific goals

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text:
This section contains how-to documentation following the Diataxis framework.

How-To Guides are task-oriented and help users accomplish specific goals: - Solve specific problems - Assume some knowledge and experience - Provide a series of steps - Focus on results

docs.title: Getting Started with the Platform
docs.text:
• Access the Workbench - Step-by-step instructions for accessing the Self-Healing Platform workbench via RHODS dashboard or terminal
  • Working with RHODS Notebooks - Guide to using Jupyter notebooks for AI/ML development, including data handling, model training, and GPU acceleration

docs.title: Deployment and Operations
docs.text: • Deploy to Production - Instructions for deploying the Self-Healing Platform to production environments

docs.title: How to Access the Self-Healing Workbench
Overview
docs.text: This guide provides comprehensive step-by-step instructions for accessing and using the Self-Healing Platform workbench through both RHODS and terminal methods. The workbench is your development environment for creating AI/ML models and self-healing algorithms.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• OpenShift Cluster Access: Access to an OpenShift 4.18+ cluster with RHODS installed
  • CLI Tools: oc CLI tool installed and configured on your local machine
  • Namespace Access: User permissions for the self-healing-platform namespace
  • Browser: Modern web browser for RHODS dashboard access (optional for terminal method)

docs.title: Method 1: RHODS Dashboard Access (Recommended for Web Users)
Step 1: Access the RHODS Dashboard
docs.text:
Navigate to the RHODS dashboard URL:

https://rhods-dashboard-redhat-ods-applications.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/

Log in with your OpenShift credentials. You'll see the RHODS home page with available workbenches.

docs.title: Step 2: Locate the Self-Healing Workbench
docs.text:
In the RHODS dashboard:

  1. Look for "self-healing-workbench-dev" in the workbench list
  2. Check the status indicator:
  3. Green (Running): Workbench is ready to use
  4. Gray (Stopped): Workbench needs to be started
  5. Yellow (Starting): Workbench is initializing

docs.title: Step 3: Start the Workbench (if needed)
docs.text:
If the workbench is stopped:

  1. Click the Start button next to the workbench
  2. Wait for the status to change to Running (typically 1-2 minutes)
  3. You'll see a Connect button appear when ready

docs.title: Step 4: Connect via Web Interface
docs.text:
Click the Connect button to open JupyterLab:

  1. The JupyterLab interface will load in a new tab
  2. You'll see the file browser on the left
  3. The terminal and notebook editors are available in the main area

Note: The web interface may have configuration complexities with the PyTorch base image. If you experience issues, use terminal access (Method 2) instead.

docs.title: Method 2: Terminal Access via oc CLI (Recommended for Power Users)
docs.text: Terminal access provides immediate productivity and full control over your development environment.

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Workbench Status
docs.text:
Check if the workbench pod is running:

```bash

docs.title: List workbench pods
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform | grep workbench

docs.title: self-healing-workbench-dev-0   2/2     Running     0          10m
docs.text:
```

If the pod is not running, check the notebook resource:

```bash

docs.title: Check notebook status
docs.text: oc get notebook -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Get detailed information
docs.text: oc describe notebook self-healing-workbench-dev -n self-healing-platform ```

docs.title: Step 2: Connect to the Workbench Terminal
docs.text:
Open a terminal connection to the workbench:

```bash

docs.title: Connect to the workbench container
docs.text:
oc exec -it self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -c self-healing-workbench \ -n self-healing-platform -- /bin/bash ```

You should see a prompt like: (base) 1001@self-healing-workbench-dev-0:/opt/app-root/src$

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Your Environment
docs.text:
Verify that all required components are available:

```bash

docs.title: Check Python version
docs.text: python --version

docs.title: Check PyTorch installation
docs.text: python -c "import torch; print(f'PyTorch: {torch.version}')"

docs.title: Check available packages
docs.text: pip list | grep -E "(torch|numpy|pandas|scikit-learn|prometheus)"

docs.title: Verify mounted volumes
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/

docs.title: Should show: data/, models/, and other directories
Step 4: Verify GPU Access (Optional)
docs.text:
If GPU nodes are available:

```bash

docs.title: Check CUDA availability
docs.text: python -c "import torch; print(f'CUDA available: {torch.cuda.is_available()}')"

docs.title: Check GPU count
docs.text: python -c "import torch; print(f'GPU count: {torch.cuda.device_count()}')"

docs.title: Test GPU tensor creation
docs.text: python -c " import torch if torch.cuda.is_available(): x = torch.randn(3, 3).cuda() print(f'GPU tensor created: {x.device}') else: print('CUDA not available - CPU mode') " ```

docs.title: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
Once connected to the workbench, clone the Self-Healing Platform repository:

```bash

docs.title: Inside the workbench terminal
docs.text: git clone https://gitea-with-admin-gitea.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/user1/openshift-aiops-platform.git

docs.title: Navigate to the project directory
docs.text: cd openshift-aiops-platform

docs.title: Verify the clone
docs.text: ls -la ```

docs.title: Set Up Development Environment
docs.text:
Install project dependencies and create working directories:

```bash

docs.title: Install project requirements
docs.text: pip install --user -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Install additional ML libraries
docs.text: pip install --user \ statsmodels>=0.14.0 \ prophet>=1.1.4 \ tslearn>=0.6.0 \ pyod>=1.1.0 \ xgboost>=2.0.0 \ prometheus-client>=0.17.0

docs.title: Create working directories
docs.text: mkdir -p notebooks/experiments mkdir -p data/training mkdir -p data/datasets mkdir -p models/checkpoints ```

docs.title: Understand the Project Structure
docs.text:
Familiarize yourself with the repository layout:

```bash

docs.title: View the project structure
docs.text: tree -L 2 -I 'pycache|*.pyc'

docs.title: Or use ls for a simpler view
docs.text:
ls -la ```

Expected structure: openshift-aiops-platform/ ├── docs/ # Documentation and ADRs │ ├── adrs/ # Architectural Decision Records │ ├── tutorials/ # Learning guides │ ├── how-to/ # Task-oriented guides │ └── explanation/ # Conceptual documentation ├── src/ # Source code │ └── coordination-engine/ # Self-healing logic ├── k8s/ # Kubernetes manifests ├── notebooks/ # Jupyter notebooks (create this) ├── data/ # Training data (create this) ├── models/ # Trained models (create this) └── requirements.txt # Python dependencies

docs.title: Persistent Storage
docs.text: The workbench has access to persistent volumes for storing your work and data:

docs.title: Data Volume (5Gi)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Use for: Training datasets, notebooks, temporary files
Check available space
docs.text: df -h /opt/app-root/src/data

docs.title: List contents
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/data

docs.title: Create subdirectories for organization
docs.text: mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/data/notebooks mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/data/datasets mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/data/experiments ```

docs.title: Models Volume (10Gi, ReadWriteMany)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Use for: Trained models, shared artifacts, checkpoints
Note: ReadWriteMany means multiple pods can access simultaneously
Check available space
docs.text: df -h /opt/app-root/src/models

docs.title: List contents
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/models

docs.title: Create subdirectories for organization
docs.text: mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/models/checkpoints mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/models/trained mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/models/artifacts ```

docs.title: Storage Best Practices
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Monitor storage usage
docs.text: du -sh /opt/app-root/src/data/ du -sh /opt/app-root/src/models/

docs.title: Clean up old files
docs.text: find /opt/app-root/src/data -type f -mtime +30 -delete

docs.title: Archive large datasets
docs.text: tar -czf /opt/app-root/src/data/archive_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz /opt/app-root/src/data/old_datasets/ ```

docs.title: Install Additional Packages
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Install a single package in user directory
docs.text: pip install --user package_name

docs.title: Install from requirements file
docs.text: pip install --user -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Install specific version
docs.text: pip install --user package_name==1.2.3

docs.title: Upgrade a package
docs.text: pip install --user --upgrade package_name ```

docs.title: Save Your Work
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Save notebooks to persistent storage
docs.text: cp my_notebook.ipynb /opt/app-root/src/data/notebooks/

docs.title: Save trained models
docs.text: cp trained_model.pkl /opt/app-root/src/models/trained/

docs.title: Save experiment results
docs.text: cp experiment_results.json /opt/app-root/src/data/experiments/

docs.title: Backup your work
docs.text: tar -czf /opt/app-root/src/data/backup_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz \ /opt/app-root/src/data/notebooks/ \ /opt/app-root/src/models/ ```

docs.title: Start Jupyter Lab (Optional)
docs.text:
If you prefer web-based notebook development:

```bash

docs.title: Start Jupyter Lab server
docs.text: jupyter lab --ip=0.0.0.0 --port=8888 --no-browser --allow-root --allow-origin='*'

docs.title: Access via browser (from your local machine)
Forward the port: oc port-forward pod/self-healing-workbench-dev-0 8888:8888
Work with Git
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Configure git (if not already configured)
docs.text: git config --global user.name "Your Name" git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"

docs.title: Create a new branch for your work
docs.text: git checkout -b feature/my-anomaly-detector

docs.title: Commit your changes
docs.text: git add . git commit -m "Add anomaly detection model"

docs.title: Push to remote
docs.text: git push origin feature/my-anomaly-detector ```

docs.title: Issue: Workbench Pod Not Running
docs.text:
Symptoms: Pod status shows "Pending", "CrashLoopBackOff", or "Error"

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Check pod status in detail
docs.text: oc describe pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Check recent events
docs.text: oc get events -n self-healing-platform --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | head -20

docs.title: Check pod logs
docs.text: oc logs self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -c self-healing-workbench -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Restart the workbench
docs.text: oc delete pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Wait for new pod to start
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform -w ```

docs.title: Issue: Cannot Connect to Workbench
docs.text:
Symptoms: Connection timeout or "Connection refused"

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Verify pod is running
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform | grep workbench

docs.title: Check if pod is ready
docs.text: oc get pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'

docs.title: Check pod events
docs.text: oc describe pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Try port-forwarding as alternative
docs.text: oc port-forward pod/self-healing-workbench-dev-0 8888:8888 -n self-healing-platform ```

docs.title: Issue: Permission Denied Errors
docs.text:
Symptoms: "Permission denied" when accessing files or directories

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Check current user
docs.text: whoami id

docs.title: Verify volume permissions
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/

docs.title: Check file ownership
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/data/ ls -la /opt/app-root/src/models/

docs.title: Fix permissions if needed
docs.text: chmod 755 /opt/app-root/src/data chmod 755 /opt/app-root/src/models ```

docs.title: Issue: Package Installation Fails
docs.text:
Symptoms: "ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement"

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Use user installation (recommended)
docs.text: pip install --user package_name

docs.title: Check pip configuration
docs.text: pip config list

docs.title: Clear pip cache
docs.text: pip cache purge

docs.title: Upgrade pip
docs.text: pip install --user --upgrade pip

docs.title: Try installing from specific index
docs.text: pip install --user -i https://pypi.org/simple/ package_name ```

docs.title: Issue: Git Clone or Push Fails
docs.text:
Symptoms: "fatal: could not read Username" or authentication errors

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Configure git credentials
docs.text: git config --global user.name "Your Name" git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"

docs.title: For private repositories, use token authentication
docs.text: git clone https://username:token@gitea-server/repo.git

docs.title: Or use SSH (if configured)
docs.text: git clone git@gitea-server:user1/openshift-aiops-platform.git

docs.title: Check git configuration
docs.text: git config --list ```

docs.title: Issue: Out of Storage Space
docs.text:
Symptoms: "No space left on device" errors

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Check storage usage
docs.text: df -h /opt/app-root/src/

docs.title: Find large files
docs.text: find /opt/app-root/src -type f -size +100M

docs.title: Clean up old files
docs.text: find /opt/app-root/src/data -type f -mtime +30 -delete

docs.title: Archive and remove old datasets
docs.text: tar -czf /opt/app-root/src/data/archive_old.tar.gz /opt/app-root/src/data/old_data/ rm -rf /opt/app-root/src/data/old_data/ ```

docs.title: Issue: GPU Not Available
docs.text:
Symptoms: torch.cuda.is_available() returns False

Solution:

```bash

docs.title: Check if GPU nodes are available in cluster
docs.text: oc get nodes -L nvidia.com/gpu

docs.title: Check if GPU operator is installed
docs.text: oc get pods -n nvidia-gpu-operator

docs.title: Check pod GPU requests
docs.text: oc describe pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform | grep -A 5 "Limits|Requests"

docs.title: Note: GPU availability depends on cluster configuration
Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you can access the workbench, explore these resources:

  1. Getting Started Tutorial - Step-by-step introduction
  2. Workbench Development Guide - Deep dive into development
  3. Architecture Overview - Understand the system design
  4. ADR Reference - Review architectural decisions

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
If you encounter issues not covered here:

  1. Check the logs: oc logs self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -c self-healing-workbench -n self-healing-platform
  2. Review ADR-011: Self-Healing Workbench Base Image
  3. Check cluster status: oc status -n self-healing-platform
  4. Contact the platform team for additional assistance

docs.title: How-To: Deploy MCP Server and Configure OpenShift Lightspeed
Overview
docs.text: This guide provides quick deployment instructions for the Cluster Health MCP Server and its integration with OpenShift Lightspeed. For architectural details, see ADR-014.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• OpenShift 4.18+ cluster with admin access
  • Self-healing platform deployed (self-healing-platform namespace exists)
  • OpenShift Lightspeed operator installed
  • MCP server image built (via BuildConfig)

docs.title: 1. Deploy MCP Server
docs.text:
Deploy the MCP server using Kustomize:

```bash

docs.title: Development deployment (with debug logging)
docs.text: oc apply -k k8s/mcp-server/overlays/development

docs.title: OR: Production deployment
docs.text:
oc apply -k k8s/mcp-server/base ```

Verify deployment:

bash oc get deployment cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform oc get service cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: 2. LLM Provider Configuration
docs.text: OpenShift Lightspeed supports multiple LLM providers. For complete configuration details, see the official Red Hat documentation.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text:
Provider Type Secret Key Name Example Config Get API Key OpenAI openai apitoken olsconfig-openai.yaml https://platform.openai.com/api-keys Google Gemini openai (compatible) apitoken olsconfig-google.yaml https://aistudio.google.com/apikey Anthropic Claude openai (compatible) apitoken olsconfig-anthropic.yaml https://console.anthropic.com/ Red Hat AI vLLM rhelai_vllm apitoken olsconfig-rhelai-vllm.yaml Internal Red Hat AI Azure OpenAI azure_openai apitoken See official docs https://portal.azure.com IBM watsonx watsonx apitoken See official docs IBM Cloud Console

IMPORTANT: The secret key must be named apitoken (not apiKey, api_key, etc.). OpenShift Lightspeed validates this and will fail reconciliation if the key name is incorrect.

docs.title: Secret Creation Examples
docs.text: OpenAI: ```bash

docs.title: Get API key from https://platform.openai.com/api-keys
docs.text:
oc create secret generic openai-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='sk-proj-...' ```

Google Gemini: ```bash

docs.title: Ensure billing is enabled on your Google Cloud project
docs.text:
oc create secret generic google-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='AIzaSy...' ```

Anthropic Claude: ```bash

docs.title: Get API key from https://console.anthropic.com/
docs.text:
oc create secret generic anthropic-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='sk-ant-...' ```

Red Hat AI vLLM: ```bash

docs.title: For internal Red Hat AI deployments
docs.text: oc create secret generic rhelai-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='YOUR_RHELAI_TOKEN' ```

docs.title: 3. Create OLSConfig
docs.text:
Create an OLSConfig resource to integrate the MCP server with OpenShift Lightspeed. This configuration is manually customized based on your LLM provider.

IMPORTANT: See the official Red Hat OpenShift Lightspeed documentation for complete provider configuration details.

docs.title: OLSConfig Structure
docs.text:
The OLSConfig supports multiple LLM providers. Choose the configuration that matches your provider:

```yaml apiVersion: ols.openshift.io/v1alpha1 kind: OLSConfig metadata: name: cluster # MUST be 'cluster' (cluster-scoped singleton) spec: # Enable MCP Server integration featureGates: - MCPServer

# LLM Provider configuration (choose one or configure multiple) llm: providers: # OpenAI (GPT-4, GPT-4o) - name: openai type: openai url: "https://api.openai.com/v1" credentialsSecretRef: name: openai-api-key models: - name: gpt-5.2-codex # Target for complex tasks - name: gpt-5-mini # Target for default - name: gpt-4o # Legacy

  # Google Gemini (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
  - name: google
    type: openai
    url: "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai"
    credentialsSecretRef:
      name: google-api-key
    models:
      - name: gemini-3-pro-preview  #  Multimodal & Reasoning
      - name: gemini-2.5-flash      #  High speed / Low cost

  # IBM BAM (Granite models)
  - name: bam
    type: bam
    url: "https://bam-api.res.ibm.com"
    credentialsSecretRef:
      name: bam-api-key
    models:
      - name: ibm/granite-4-h-small  #  Enterprise Workhorse
      - name: ibm/granite-4-h-tiny   #  Edge/Low-Latency

  # Azure OpenAI
  - name: azure
    type: azure_openai
    url: "https://YOUR_RESOURCE.openai.azure.com"
    credentialsSecretRef:
      name: azure-openai-key
    models:
      - name: gpt-5            #  Requires matching deployment
      - name: gpt-5-mini       #  High efficiency
      - name: gpt-4.1          # [16] Stable baseline

# MCP Server configuration (integrates with self-healing platform) mcpServers: - name: cluster-health streamableHTTP: url: http://mcp-server.self-healing-platform.svc:8080/mcp timeout: 30 enableSSE: true

# OLS deployment configuration ols: defaultModel: gpt-4o-mini # Choose your default model defaultProvider: openai # Choose your default provider deployment: replicas: 1 conversationCache: type: postgres # REQUIRED: Only 'postgres' is supported redis: maxMemory: "2000mb" maxMemoryPolicy: "allkeys-lru"

# Console plugin configuration console: enabled: true ```

Apply your customized configuration:

```bash

docs.title: Save the above YAML to a file (e.g., olsconfig.yaml) with your provider details
Then apply it:
docs.text:
oc apply -f olsconfig.yaml ```

Verify configuration:

```bash

docs.title: Check OLSConfig exists (name must be 'cluster')
docs.text: oc get olsconfig cluster

docs.title: View detailed status
docs.text:
oc describe olsconfig cluster ```

Check reconciliation status: ```bash

docs.title: Watch for all conditions to become Ready
docs.text: oc get olsconfig cluster -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[].type}' && echo oc get olsconfig cluster -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[].status}' && echo

docs.title: Configuration Requirements
docs.text:
Per Red Hat OpenShift Lightspeed CRD specification:

  1. metadata.name: MUST be cluster (cluster-scoped singleton resource)
  2. conversationCache.type: MUST be postgres (only supported type, not memory)
  3. Secret key name: MUST be apitoken (not apiKey, api_key, etc.)
  4. MCP transport: MUST use streamableHTTP configuration (stdio not supported by CRD)
  5. Feature gates: Include MCPServer to enable MCP functionality
  6. Models: MUST be nested inside each provider's models array
  7. Provider types: Supported values are azure_openai, bam, openai, watsonx, rhoai_vllm, rhelai_vllm, fake_provider

docs.title: Provider-Specific Notes
docs.text:
Google Gemini: - Configured as openai type (Google provides OpenAI-compatible endpoint) - URL: https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai - Models: gemini-1.5-pro, gemini-1.5-flash, or models/gemini-3-pro-preview (experimental) - Requires Generative Language API enabled and billing configured

Anthropic Claude: - Configured as openai type (Anthropic provides OpenAI-compatible API) - URL: https://api.anthropic.com/v1 - Models: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022, claude-3-5-haiku-20241022

Red Hat AI vLLM: - Uses dedicated rhelai_vllm provider type - URL: Internal cluster service (e.g., http://vllm-service.rhelai.svc.cluster.local:8000/v1) - Models: IBM Granite models (granite-3.1-8b-instruct, granite-3.1-2b-instruct)

docs.title: 4. Test Integration
docs.text:
Test the MCP server health endpoint:

```bash

docs.title: From a test pod
docs.text:
oc run test-curl --image=registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/ubi-minimal:latest \ --rm -i --restart=Never -n self-healing-platform -- \ curl -s http://cluster-health-mcp-server:3000/health ```

Expected response:

json { "status": "healthy", "mcpServer": "cluster-health", "version": "1.0.0" }

docs.title: MCP Server Environment Variables
docs.text:
Customize the deployment by editing environment variables:

```yaml

docs.title: k8s/mcp-server/base/deployment.yaml
docs.text: env: - name: MCP_TRANSPORT value: "stdio" # or "http" for HTTP transport - name: COORDINATION_ENGINE_URL value: "http://coordination-engine:8080" - name: LOG_LEVEL value: "info" # or "debug" for detailed logs - name: NODE_ENV value: "production" ```

docs.title: OLSConfig Options
docs.text:
The OLSConfig supports multiple LLM providers:

```yaml spec: llm: providers: # OpenAI (default) - name: openai type: openai url: "https://api.openai.com/v1" credentialsSecretRef: name: openai-api-key

  # IBM BAM (Granite models)
  - name: bam
    type: bam
    url: "https://bam-api.res.ibm.com"
    credentialsSecretRef:
      name: bam-api-key

  # Azure OpenAI
  - name: azure
    type: azure_openai
    url: "https://YOUR_RESOURCE.openai.azure.com"
    credentialsSecretRef:
      name: azure-openai-key

models:
  - name: gpt-4o-mini      # Recommended for cost/performance
    provider: openai
  - name: gpt-4o           # For complex reasoning
    provider: openai
  - name: granite-13b-chat-v2  # Open source option
    provider: bam

ols: defaultModel: gpt-4o-mini # Choose your default defaultProvider: openai ```

docs.title: MCP Server Tools and Resources
docs.text:
The MCP server exposes these capabilities to OpenShift Lightspeed:

Tools (Actions): - trigger-remediation - Initiate self-healing actions - analyze-anomalies - Run anomaly detection analysis - get-cluster-health - Retrieve current cluster health status - query-incidents - Search incident history

Resources (Data): - cluster://health - Real-time cluster health metrics - cluster://incidents - Incident history and details - cluster://nodes - Node health and status - cluster://anomalies - Detected anomalies

docs.title: Ask OpenShift Lightspeed
docs.text:
Once configured, you can ask Lightspeed natural language questions:

``` You: "Show me the current cluster health" Lightspeed: Uses MCP server's cluster://health resource

You: "What incidents happened in the last hour?" Lightspeed: Uses query-incidents tool

You: "Trigger remediation for incident INC-12345" Lightspeed: Uses trigger-remediation tool ```

docs.title: Programmatic Access (from Notebooks)
docs.text: ```python import requests

docs.title: MCP server health
docs.text: response = requests.get('http://cluster-health-mcp-server.self-healing-platform.svc:3000/health') print(response.json())

docs.title: OpenShift Lightspeed query (requires auth token)
docs.text: import openai openai.api_base = "https://lightspeed-console-openshift-lightspeed.apps.YOUR_CLUSTER/api" response = openai.ChatCompletion.create( model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Show cluster health"}] ) print(response.choices[0].message.content) ```

docs.title: MCP Server Not Starting
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check pod status
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform -l app=cluster-health-mcp-server

docs.title: Check logs
docs.text: oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform --tail=50

docs.title: 1. Image not found - ensure BuildConfig completed
docs.text: oc get build -n self-healing-platform | grep mcp-server

docs.title: 2. ServiceAccount missing - create if needed
docs.text: oc get sa self-healing-operator -n self-healing-platform ```

docs.title: OpenShift Lightspeed Not Connecting
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check OLSConfig status
docs.text: oc get olsconfig cluster-health-assistant -o yaml

docs.title: Verify MCP server service
docs.text: oc get service cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Test connectivity from Lightspeed namespace
docs.text: oc run test-curl -n openshift-lightspeed --image=curlimages/curl:latest \ --rm -i --restart=Never -- \ curl -v http://cluster-health-mcp-server.self-healing-platform.svc:3000/health ```

docs.title: LLM Provider Issues
docs.text:
Error: "missing key 'apitoken'"

This is the most common error. The secret must have a key named apitoken.

```bash

docs.title: Check current secret keys
docs.text: oc get secret google-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq

docs.title: If it shows "apiKey" instead of "apitoken", fix it:
1. Get the current value
docs.text: API_KEY=$(oc get secret google-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed -o jsonpath='{.data.apiKey}' | base64 -d)

docs.title: 2. Delete and recreate with correct key
docs.text:
oc delete secret google-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed oc create secret generic google-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed --from-literal=apitoken="$API_KEY" ```

Error: "Unsupported value: \"google\""

Google is not a native provider type. Use type: openai with Google's OpenAI-compatible endpoint: yaml providers: - name: google-gemini type: openai url: "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai"

Error: "Unsupported value: \"memory\""

Only postgres is supported for conversation cache: yaml conversationCache: type: postgres

Error: "Required value for field spec.llm.providers[0].models"

Models must be inside each provider configuration: yaml providers: - name: google-gemini type: openai models: # ✅ Inside provider - name: gemini-1.5-pro

General Debugging: ```bash

docs.title: Check API key secret
docs.text: oc get secret google-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed -o yaml

docs.title: Verify OLSConfig provider configuration
docs.text: oc get olsconfig cluster -o jsonpath='{.spec.llm.providers}' | jq .

docs.title: Check reconciliation status and errors
docs.text: oc get olsconfig cluster -o yaml | grep -A 10 "conditions:"

docs.title: Check lightspeed-app-server logs for LLM connection errors
docs.text: oc logs -n openshift-lightspeed deployment/lightspeed-app-server -c lightspeed-service-api --tail=50 | grep -i "llm|error" ```

docs.title: Provider-Specific Issues
docs.text:
For detailed provider configuration, see Red Hat OpenShift Lightspeed documentation.

OpenAI:

Error: API key not valid ```bash

docs.title: Check key format (should start with 'sk-proj-' or 'sk-')
docs.text: oc get secret openai-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed -o jsonpath='{.data.apitoken}' | base64 -d

docs.title: Recreate secret if needed
docs.text:
oc delete secret openai-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed oc create secret generic openai-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='sk-proj-YOUR_KEY_HERE' ```

Error: Rate limit exceeded - Upgrade to paid tier at https://platform.openai.com/account/billing - Or wait for rate limit reset (depends on your plan) - Consider using gpt-4o-mini for lower costs

Google Gemini:

Error: API key not valid. Please pass a valid API key ```bash

docs.title: 3. Ensure billing is enabled on your Google Cloud project
4. Recreate secret with correct API key (starts with 'AIzaSy')
docs.text: oc delete secret google-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed oc create secret generic google-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='AIzaSyXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX'

docs.title: Restart lightspeed pods to pick up new key
docs.text:
oc rollout restart deployment lightspeed-app-server -n openshift-lightspeed ```

Error: Model not found - Google Gemini models via OpenAI-compatible endpoint: gemini-1.5-pro, gemini-1.5-flash - Experimental models may require models/ prefix: models/gemini-3-pro-preview - Check available models at: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models/gemini

Configuration: - Provider type: openai (not google) - URL: https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai

Anthropic Claude:

Error: Invalid API key ```bash

docs.title: API key format: starts with 'sk-ant-'
docs.text:
oc delete secret anthropic-api-key -n openshift-lightspeed oc create secret generic anthropic-api-key \ -n openshift-lightspeed \ --from-literal=apitoken='sk-ant-YOUR_KEY_HERE' ```

Error: Model not found - Anthropic Claude models: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022, claude-3-5-haiku-20241022, claude-3-opus-20240229 - Use full model name with date suffix - Check latest models at: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models

Configuration: - Provider type: openai (Anthropic supports OpenAI-compatible API) - URL: https://api.anthropic.com/v1

Red Hat AI vLLM:

Error: Connection refused or Service not found ```bash

docs.title: Check vLLM service exists
docs.text: oc get service vllm-service -n rhelai

docs.title: Verify service URL in OLSConfig matches actual deployment
docs.text: oc get olsconfig cluster -o jsonpath='{.spec.llm.providers[?(@.name=="rhelai-vllm")].url}'

docs.title: Test connectivity from lightspeed namespace
docs.text:
oc run test-curl -n openshift-lightspeed --image=curlimages/curl:latest \ --rm -i --restart=Never -- \ curl -v http://vllm-service.rhelai.svc.cluster.local:8000/v1/models ```

Error: Model not found - Verify model name matches deployed model in vLLM service - Common IBM Granite models: granite-3.1-8b-instruct, granite-3.1-2b-instruct - Check deployed models: bash oc exec -n rhelai <vllm-pod> -- curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models

Configuration: - Provider type: rhelai_vllm (specific type for Red Hat AI) - URL: Internal cluster service (e.g., http://vllm-service.rhelai.svc.cluster.local:8000/v1) - Requires internal Red Hat AI deployment

Azure OpenAI:

For Azure OpenAI configuration, see Red Hat official documentation.

IBM watsonx:

For IBM watsonx configuration, see Red Hat official documentation.

docs.title: Interacting with MCP Server via OpenShift Lightspeed
Overview
docs.text: Once the MCP server is integrated with OpenShift Lightspeed, you can interact with your self-healing platform using natural language queries. The MCP server exposes cluster health data, incident management, and remediation tools through conversational AI.

docs.title: Accessing OpenShift Lightspeed
docs.text:
Option 1: OpenShift Console 1. Navigate to OpenShift Console → Help (top right) 2. Click OpenShift Lightspeed or the chatbot icon 3. Start asking questions about your cluster

Option 2: Command Line (if CLI integration available) ```bash

docs.title: Check if Lightspeed CLI is available
docs.text: oc lightspeed --help

docs.title: Example query
docs.text: oc lightspeed query "What is the current cluster health status?" ```

docs.title: Example Queries and MCP Tools
docs.text: The MCP server provides several tools and resources. Here's how to interact with them:

docs.title: 1. Cluster Health Queries
docs.text:
Query: "What is the current health status of my cluster?"

MCP Tool Used: get-cluster-health

Response Example: The cluster is healthy with: - 7 nodes (3 control-plane, 4 workers) - 95% node availability - 2 active InferenceServices - 1 coordination engine running - No critical alerts

Related Notebook: notebooks/00-setup/00-platform-readiness-validation.ipynb - Update When: Health checks fail, new validation criteria needed - What to Update: Add new validation functions, update health scoring logic

docs.title: 2. Incident and Anomaly Queries
docs.text:
Query: "Show me recent anomalies detected in the last hour"

MCP Tool Used: query-incidents

Response Example: ``` Found 3 anomalies in the last hour: 1. High CPU usage on worker-2 (95% @ 14:23 UTC) 2. Memory pressure on predictive-analytics pod (87% @ 14:45 UTC) 3. Increased API latency in coordination-engine (450ms @ 15:01 UTC)

All anomalies are being monitored. No immediate action required. ```

Related Notebooks: - notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb - notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/02-time-series-anomaly-detection.ipynb

Update When: - False positives/negatives detected - New anomaly patterns emerge - Model accuracy drops below threshold

What to Update: - Adjust isolation forest contamination parameter - Retrain time-series models with recent data - Update threshold values based on observed patterns

docs.title: 3. Remediation Workflow Queries
docs.text:
Query: "Trigger remediation for the high memory usage anomaly"

MCP Tool Used: trigger-remediation

Response Example: ``` Remediation initiated for incident #1234: - Type: Memory pressure - Target: predictive-analytics-predictor - Action: Scale down replicas and restart - Status: In progress - Estimated completion: 2 minutes

Tracking remediation workflow... ```

Related Notebooks: - notebooks/03-self-healing-logic/coordination-engine-integration.ipynb - notebooks/03-self-healing-logic/hybrid-healing-workflows.ipynb

Update When: - Remediation actions fail or incomplete - New remediation strategies needed - Coordination engine logic requires tuning

What to Update: - Add new remediation action types - Update coordination engine decision logic - Adjust priority scoring for conflicts

docs.title: 4. Model Serving Health Queries
docs.text:
Query: "How are my InferenceServices performing?"

MCP Resource Used: cluster://anomalies

Response Example: ``` InferenceService Status: 1. predictive-analytics: Ready (2 replicas, 99.5% uptime) - Average latency: 45ms - Requests/min: 120 - Error rate: 0.1%

  1. anomaly-detector: Ready (1 replica, 98.2% uptime)
  2. Average latency: 78ms
  3. Requests/min: 85
  4. Error rate: 0.3% ```

Related Notebooks: - notebooks/04-model-serving/kserve-model-deployment.ipynb - notebooks/07-monitoring-operations/model-performance-monitoring.ipynb

Update When: - Model performance degrades - Latency increases beyond SLA - Error rates spike

What to Update: - Retrain models with recent data - Adjust KServe autoscaling configuration - Optimize model inference code - Update model serving resources (CPU/memory)

docs.title: 5. Advanced Analytics Queries
docs.text:
Query: "Run anomaly analysis on the past week's metrics"

MCP Tool Used: analyze-anomalies

Response Example: ``` Weekly Anomaly Analysis (Dec 2-9, 2025): - Total anomalies detected: 47 - Critical: 3, High: 12, Medium: 18, Low: 14 - Most common: Resource exhaustion (18 instances) - Peak anomaly times: 14:00-16:00 UTC - Accuracy: 94% (based on validated incidents)

Recommendations: 1. Scale up during peak hours (14:00-16:00) 2. Review resource limits for high-usage pods 3. Consider adding predictive scaling policies ```

Related Notebooks: - notebooks/05-end-to-end-scenarios/resource-exhaustion-detection.ipynb - notebooks/05-end-to-end-scenarios/pod-crash-loop-healing.ipynb

Update When: - Analysis reveals new patterns - Recommendations not actionable - Accuracy drops below 90%

What to Update: - Enhance feature engineering - Add new anomaly detection algorithms - Update recommendation logic - Create new end-to-end scenario notebooks

docs.title: Iterative Improvement Workflow
docs.text:
Use this cycle to continuously improve your notebooks based on OpenShift Lightspeed interactions:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 1. Interact with OpenShift Lightspeed │ │ └─> Ask questions, trigger actions │ └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 2. Monitor MCP Server Logs │ │ └─> oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp- │ │ server -n self-healing-platform │ └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 3. Identify Gaps or Issues │ │ - Inaccurate responses │ │ - Missing data or tools │ │ - Poor model performance │ │ - Failed remediation actions │ └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 4. Update Corresponding Notebooks │ │ └─> See "Related Notebooks" above │ └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 5. Run Updated Notebooks │ │ - Train new models │ │ - Update logic/thresholds │ │ - Deploy via NotebookValidationJob │ └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 6. Verify Improvements │ │ └─> Re-test with OpenShift Lightspeed │ └────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └──────> Repeat ───────────────────────┘

docs.title: Monitoring MCP Server Interactions
docs.text: Check MCP Server Logs: ```bash

docs.title: Real-time log streaming
docs.text: oc logs -f deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Filter for tool invocations
docs.text: oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform \ | grep "Tool invoked"

docs.title: Check for errors
docs.text:
oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform \ | grep -i error | tail -20 ```

Log Patterns to Watch: - Tool invoked: <tool-name> - Which tools are being used - Response time: <ms> - Performance metrics - Error: - Failed tool invocations or data access issues - Model inference failed - Model serving problems

docs.title: Notebook Update Decision Matrix
docs.text:
Use this matrix to decide which notebooks to update based on MCP server interactions:

Issue Observed Notebook to Update Priority Health check failure 00-setup/00-platform-readiness-validation.ipynb HIGH False positive anomaly 02-anomaly-detection/01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb HIGH Missed anomaly 02-anomaly-detection/02-time-series-anomaly-detection.ipynb HIGH Remediation failure 03-self-healing-logic/coordination-engine-integration.ipynb CRITICAL Model serving timeout 04-model-serving/kserve-model-deployment.ipynb HIGH Slow inference 07-monitoring-operations/model-performance-monitoring.ipynb MEDIUM Inaccurate recommendations 05-end-to-end-scenarios/*.ipynb MEDIUM New use case identified Create new notebook in appropriate category MEDIUM

docs.title: Example: Complete Interaction → Notebook Update Cycle
docs.text:
Step 1: User Query User: "Why is the predictive-analytics pod restarting frequently?"

Step 2: MCP Server Response Lightspeed: "The predictive-analytics pod has restarted 12 times in the last hour due to memory pressure (OOMKilled). Current memory usage: 1.8GB / 2GB limit."

Step 3: Identify Gap - Memory limit too low for current workload - No proactive detection of memory pressure before OOM

Step 4: Update Notebooks 1. 02-anomaly-detection/03-lstm-based-prediction.ipynb - Add memory pressure detection - Train model to predict OOM before it happens - Adjust warning thresholds

  1. 05-end-to-end-scenarios/resource-exhaustion-detection.ipynb
  2. Add memory pressure scenario

  3. Implement proactive scaling logic

  4. 04-model-serving/kserve-model-deployment.ipynb

  5. Update resource requests/limits based on actual usage
  6. Add autoscaling based on memory metrics

Step 5: Deploy Updates ```bash

docs.title: Run validation jobs to retrain models
docs.text: oc get notebookvalidationjob -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Or manually run in workbench
docs.text: oc port-forward self-healing-workbench-0 8888:8888 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Open notebooks and execute
docs.text:
```

Step 6: Verify User: "Are there any memory pressure issues now?" Lightspeed: "No memory pressure detected. All pods are within 70% of memory limits. Proactive scaling recommendations are now available in the coordination engine."

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Explore MCP Tools: Try different tools via Lightspeed queries
  • Monitor Interactions: Track which queries are most common
  • Iterative Improvement: Use the workflow above to enhance notebooks
  • Scale: Increase replicas for high-traffic environments

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• ADR-014: Cluster Health MCP Server - Architecture and design decisions
  • Notebook: MCP Server Integration - Development and testing
  • Notebook: OpenShift Lightspeed Integration - Advanced usage
  • MCP Protocol Documentation - Upstream MCP specification

docs.title: Support
docs.text: For issues, questions, or contributions: - Check existing ADRs in docs/adrs/ - Review notebooks in notebooks/06-mcp-lightspeed-integration/ - Open an issue in the project repository

docs.title: Deploying on Single Node OpenShift (SNO)
docs.text: This guide explains how to deploy the AI Ops Self-Healing Platform on Single Node OpenShift (SNO) clusters.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Prerequisites
  • Deployment Steps
  • Limitations
  • Troubleshooting
  • Best Practices

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
Minimum: - 8 vCPUs - 32GB RAM - 120GB disk

Recommended: - 16 vCPUs - 64GB RAM - 500GB disk

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
• OpenShift 4.18, 4.19, or 4.20
  • SingleReplica topology (controlPlaneTopology=SingleReplica, infrastructureTopology=SingleReplica)
  • CSI storage classes available (gp2-csi, gp3-csi for AWS)

docs.title: Verify Cluster Topology
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check control plane topology
docs.text: oc get infrastructure cluster -o jsonpath='{.status.controlPlaneTopology}'

docs.title: Check infrastructure topology
docs.text: oc get infrastructure cluster -o jsonpath='{.status.infrastructureTopology}'

docs.title: Check platform type
docs.text: oc get infrastructure cluster -o jsonpath='{.status.platformStatus.type}'

docs.title: Check Storage Classes
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: List available storage classes
docs.text: oc get sc

docs.title: Ensure a default CSI storage class exists
docs.text: oc get sc -o jsonpath='{.items[?(@.metadata.annotations.storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class=="true")].metadata.name}'

docs.title: 1. Clone Repository
docs.text: bash git clone https://github.com/KubeHeal/openshift-aiops-platform.git cd openshift-aiops-platform

docs.title: 2. Login to OpenShift
docs.text: bash oc login --token=<your-token> --server=https://api.<cluster-domain>:6443

docs.title: 3. Verify Cluster Detection
docs.text:
bash make show-cluster-info

Expected Output: ``` Cluster Information: Topology: sno OpenShift Version: 4.20 Platform: AWS ODF Channel: stable-4.20

Cluster Topology Information: Type: SNO (Single Node OpenShift) Control Plane: SingleReplica Infrastructure: SingleReplica Platform: AWS ```

docs.title: 4. Configure Cluster Infrastructure
docs.text:
bash make configure-cluster

Expected Behavior: - ✅ Skips MachineSet scaling (log message: "Skipping Worker Node Scaling (SNO Cluster)") - ✅ Installs ODF operator (MCG-only mode, no Ceph daemons) - ✅ Creates MCG-only StorageCluster (NooBaa S3 with gp3-csi backing) - ✅ Waits for NooBaa to become Ready - ✅ Validates storage classes

docs.title: 5. Build Execution Environment (First Time Only)
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Set your Ansible Hub token
docs.text: export ANSIBLE_HUB_TOKEN=

docs.title: Or create a token file
docs.text: echo "" > token

docs.title: Build the execution environment
docs.text: make build-ee ```

docs.title: 6. Deploy Pattern
docs.text:
Option 1: Auto-detection (Recommended)

bash make operator-deploy

The platform will automatically detect SNO topology and adjust deployment accordingly.

Option 2: Explicit SNO Configuration

Edit values-hub.yaml before deploying:

```yaml cluster: topology: "sno"

storage: modelStorage: storageClass: "gp3-csi" # CSI only (no CephFS on SNO)

docs.title: objectStore.enabled stays true -- MCG-only ODF provides NooBaa S3
docs.text:
```

Then deploy:

bash make operator-deploy

docs.title: 7. Validate Deployment
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check ArgoCD application health
docs.text: make argo-healthcheck

docs.title: Verify pods are running
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Check PVCs (should use CSI storage classes)
docs.text: oc get pvc -n self-healing-platform ```

docs.title: Storage
docs.text: ⚠️ MCG-only ODF (no full Ceph) - NooBaa S3 object storage is available (via MCG-only StorageCluster) - No CephFS or RBD storage classes - Only CSI storage classes for block/file (gp2-csi, gp3-csi on AWS) - ReadWriteMany (RWX) support depends on CSI driver

docs.title: Resource Constraints
docs.text: ⚠️ All workloads compete for single node's resources - Reduced resource limits should be configured in values-hub.yaml - GPU workloads may impact cluster stability - Monitor resource usage closely

docs.title: High Availability
docs.text: ❌ No HA capabilities - Single point of failure - Node maintenance requires cluster downtime - Not recommended for production workloads requiring HA

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text: ❌ Cannot add worker nodes - No MachineSet scaling support - Vertical scaling only (resize the node) - Horizontal pod autoscaling limited by single node capacity

docs.title: Issue: ODF Installation Attempted
docs.text:
Symptom: Script tries to install ODF, fails due to insufficient nodes

Error: ODF requires minimum 3 nodes

Solution: bash export CLUSTER_TOPOLOGY=sno make configure-cluster

docs.title: Issue: Pods Pending Due to Resources
docs.text:
Symptom: Pods stuck in Pending state with "Insufficient memory/cpu" errors

Warning FailedScheduling pod/my-pod 0/1 nodes available: insufficient memory

Solution: Reduce resource requests/limits in values-hub.yaml notebook validation resources, then redeploy:

bash make operator-deploy

docs.title: Issue: Storage Class Not Found (ocs-storagecluster-cephfs)
docs.text:
Symptom: PVCs pending, looking for ODF storage classes

Warning ProvisioningFailed persistentvolumeclaim/model-storage-pvc storageclass.storage.k8s.io "ocs-storagecluster-cephfs" not found

Solution: Ensure CSI storage is default and update values

```bash

docs.title: Set gp3-csi as default storage class
docs.text: oc patch storageclass gp3-csi -p '{"metadata": {"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'

docs.title: Remove default from other storage classes
docs.text: oc patch storageclass gp2-csi -p '{"metadata": {"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"false"}}}'

docs.title: Redeploy after updating values-hub.yaml with SNO settings
docs.text: make operator-deploy ```

docs.title: Issue: Auto-Detection Not Working
docs.text:
Symptom: Platform detects "ha" topology on SNO cluster

Solution: Manually override

```bash export CLUSTER_TOPOLOGY=sno make show-cluster-info # Verify detection

docs.title: Then edit values-hub.yaml: set cluster.topology to "sno", storage.modelStorage.storageClass to "gp3-csi"
docs.text: make operator-deploy ```

docs.title: Issue: Notebook Validation Jobs Failing
docs.text:
Symptom: NotebookValidationJob pods OOMKilled or CrashLoopBackOff

Solution: Reduce notebook resource limits in values-hub.yaml under notebooks.validation.resources:

yaml notebooks: validation: resources: tier1: limits: memory: "256Mi" # Reduced from 1Gi cpu: "250m" # Reduced from 1000m

docs.title: 1. Use CSI Storage
docs.text:
Always use CSI-based storage classes (gp3-csi recommended for AWS):

yaml storage: modelStorage: storageClass: "gp3-csi" modelStorageGpu: storageClass: "gp3-csi"

docs.title: 2. Monitor Resources
docs.text:
Single node means shared resources - monitor closely:

```bash

docs.title: Watch node resource usage
docs.text: watch 'oc adm top node'

docs.title: Watch pod resource usage
docs.text: watch 'oc adm top pods -n self-healing-platform'

docs.title: Check resource requests/limits
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,CPU_REQ:.spec.containers[].resources.requests.cpu,MEM_REQ:.spec.containers[].resources.requests.memory,CPU_LIM:.spec.containers[].resources.limits.cpu,MEM_LIM:.spec.containers[].resources.limits.memory' ```

docs.title: 3. Limit Workloads
docs.text:
Deploy only essential workloads on SNO:

  • ✅ Core coordination engine
  • ✅ Essential notebooks (tier1, tier2)
  • ⚠️ Advanced notebooks (tier3) - be cautious
  • ❌ Large-scale model training - not recommended

docs.title: 4. Set Appropriate Resource Requests/Limits
docs.text:
Configure SNO settings directly in values-hub.yaml and adjust as needed:

```bash

docs.title: Edit values-hub.yaml with SNO overrides, then deploy
docs.text: make operator-deploy ```

docs.title: 5. Regular Backups
docs.text:
Critical for SNO: Single node = single point of failure

```bash

docs.title: Backup etcd regularly
docs.text: oc get nodes oc debug node/

docs.title: 6. Plan Maintenance Windows
docs.text:
Node maintenance requires cluster downtime:

  • Schedule maintenance during off-hours
  • Notify users in advance
  • Test upgrades in dev/test SNO first

docs.title: 7. Consider Upgrade Path
docs.text:
For Production: Plan migration to HA (HighlyAvailable) cluster:

  1. Deploy HA cluster with 3+ nodes
  2. Migrate workloads using GitOps (ArgoCD)
  3. Update values-hub.yaml to use ODF storage classes
  4. Redeploy with make operator-deploy

docs.title: SNO Overrides in values-hub.yaml
docs.text:
Apply these changes to values-hub.yaml for SNO deployments:

```yaml

docs.title: Cluster configuration
docs.text: cluster: topology: "sno"

docs.title: Storage classes (CSI for block/file - MCG provides S3)
docs.text: storage: modelStorage: size: "10Gi" storageClass: "gp3-csi" # Changed from ocs-storagecluster-cephfs

docs.title: Object store stays enabled -- MCG-only ODF provides NooBaa S3
docs.text: objectStore: enabled: true

docs.title: Optionally reduce notebook resource limits

docs.title: How-To: Using OpenShift Lightspeed Interactions to Improve Notebooks
Overview
docs.text: This guide explains how to use your interactions with OpenShift Lightspeed (via the MCP server) as feedback to continuously improve your Jupyter notebooks. This creates a virtuous cycle where real-world usage directly enhances your AI/ML models and self-healing logic.

docs.title: The Feedback Loop
docs.text: OpenShift Lightspeed Query ↓ MCP Server Response ↓ Identify Gaps/Issues ↓ Update Relevant Notebooks ↓ Retrain/Redeploy Models ↓ Improved Lightspeed Responses ↓ (Repeat)

docs.title: Quick Reference Table
docs.text: MCP Tool/Resource Purpose Related Notebooks Update Trigger get-cluster-health Cluster status overview 00-setup/00-platform-readiness-validation.ipynb Health checks fail query-incidents List anomalies/incidents 02-anomaly-detection/*.ipynb False positives/negatives analyze-anomalies Run anomaly analysis 02-anomaly-detection/*.ipynb05-end-to-end-scenarios/*.ipynb Poor accuracy, new patterns trigger-remediation Execute healing actions 03-self-healing-logic/*.ipynb Remediation fails cluster://health Health metrics resource 00-setup/*.ipynb07-monitoring-operations/*.ipynb Missing metrics cluster://incidents Incident data resource 02-anomaly-detection/*.ipynb Incomplete incident data cluster://nodes Node status resource 00-setup/*.ipynb Node health issues cluster://anomalies Anomaly data resource 02-anomaly-detection/*.ipynb04-model-serving/*.ipynb Model performance issues

docs.title: Step 1: Interact with OpenShift Lightspeed
docs.text:
Access Lightspeed: - OpenShift Console → Help → OpenShift Lightspeed - Or use CLI: oc lightspeed query "your question"

Example Queries: "What is the current cluster health?" "Show me anomalies from the last 24 hours" "Why is the predictive-analytics pod failing?" "Trigger remediation for high CPU usage on worker-2" "How are my InferenceServices performing?"

docs.title: Step 2: Monitor MCP Server Logs
docs.text: Real-time Monitoring: ```bash

docs.title: Stream logs
docs.text: oc logs -f deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Watch for tool invocations
docs.text: oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform \ --since=1h | grep "Tool invoked"

docs.title: Check response times
docs.text:
oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform \ --since=1h | grep "Response time" ```

Key Log Patterns: ```json // Tool invocation {"level":"info","message":"Tool invoked: query-incidents","params":{"time_range":"1h"},"timestamp":"..."}

// Successful response {"level":"info","message":"Tool response","tool":"query-incidents","duration":"234ms","success":true}

// Error (needs attention!) {"level":"error","message":"Tool execution failed","tool":"analyze-anomalies","error":"Model inference timeout"} ```

docs.title: Step 3: Identify Issues and Gaps
docs.text:
Common Issues to Watch For:

Issue Symptom Impact Inaccurate Responses Wrong cluster status, incorrect metrics Low trust, wrong decisions Missing Data "No data available" responses Limited usefulness Slow Responses >5 second response times Poor user experience Failed Tool Invocations Errors in logs, incomplete actions Broken functionality False Positives Anomalies that aren't real issues Alert fatigue False Negatives Real issues not detected Unaddressed problems Failed Remediation Healing actions don't complete Persistent issues

Example: Identifying a Gap ``` User Query: "Are there any memory leaks in my cluster?" Lightspeed Response: "No anomalies detected"

[Reality: predictive-analytics pod has been slowly leaking memory for 3 days]

GAP IDENTIFIED: - Time-series anomaly detection not catching gradual memory leaks - Need to update LSTM model with long-term trend detection ```

docs.title: A. Anomaly Detection Improvements
docs.text:
Notebook: notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb

When to Update: - False positives (normal behavior flagged as anomaly) - False negatives (real issues missed) - New anomaly patterns discovered

What to Update: ```python

docs.title: Adjust contamination parameter based on false positive rate
docs.text: isolation_forest = IsolationForest( contamination=0.02, # Reduce if too many false positives random_state=42 )

docs.title: Add new features based on missed anomalies
docs.text:
features = [ 'cpu_usage', 'memory_usage', 'memory_growth_rate', # NEW: catch memory leaks 'network_throughput', 'api_latency_p95' # NEW: detect performance degradation ] ```

Retrain: ```bash

docs.title: Run via NotebookValidationJob
docs.text: oc get notebookvalidationjob -n self-healing-platform \ -l notebook=01-isolation-forest-implementation

docs.title: Or manually in workbench
docs.text: oc port-forward self-healing-workbench-0 8888:8888 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Execute notebook cells to retrain with new features
B. Time-Series Analysis Updates
docs.text:
Notebook: notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/02-time-series-anomaly-detection.ipynb

When to Update: - Gradual trends not detected (e.g., memory leaks) - Seasonal patterns missed - Model accuracy below 90%

What to Update: ```python

docs.title: Add longer time window for trend detection
docs.text: prophet_model = Prophet( changepoint_prior_scale=0.05, seasonality_prior_scale=10.0, seasonality_mode='multiplicative', # NEW: longer interval for detecting slow leaks interval_width=0.95, daily_seasonality=True, weekly_seasonality=True )

docs.title: Add custom seasonality
docs.text: prophet_model.add_seasonality( name='business_hours', period=1, fourier_order=5, mode='additive' ) ```

docs.title: C. Self-Healing Logic Enhancements
docs.text:
Notebook: notebooks/03-self-healing-logic/coordination-engine-integration.ipynb

When to Update: - Remediation actions fail - Wrong action chosen for incident - Conflicts between remediation strategies

What to Update: ```python

docs.title: Update remediation action priority scoring
docs.text:
def calculate_action_priority(anomaly): priority_score = 0

# Severity weight
severity_weights = {
    'critical': 100,
    'high': 75,
    'medium': 50,
    'low': 25
}
priority_score += severity_weights.get(anomaly['severity'], 0)

# NEW: Add success rate weight based on past performance
action_success_rate = get_historical_success_rate(anomaly['type'])
priority_score *= action_success_rate

# NEW: Add impact assessment
if anomaly['affects_user_traffic']:
    priority_score *= 1.5

return priority_score

docs.title: Update coordination engine API call
docs.text: response = coordination_engine_client.submit_anomaly({ 'timestamp': anomaly['timestamp'], 'type': anomaly['type'], 'severity': anomaly['severity'], 'recommended_action': 'scale_up', # Updated based on failure analysis 'confidence_score': 0.95, 'priority_score': calculate_action_priority(anomaly) }) ```

docs.title: D. Model Serving Optimization
docs.text:
Notebook: notebooks/04-model-serving/kserve-model-deployment.ipynb

When to Update: - Inference timeouts - High error rates - Insufficient throughput

What to Update: ```python

docs.title: Update InferenceService configuration
docs.text: inference_service = V1beta1InferenceService( api_version="serving.kserve.io/v1beta1", kind="InferenceService", metadata=client.V1ObjectMeta( name="predictive-analytics", namespace="self-healing-platform" ), spec=V1beta1InferenceServiceSpec( predictor=V1beta1PredictorSpec( sklearn=V1beta1SKLearnSpec( storage_uri="pvc://model-storage-pvc/predictive-analytics", # NEW: Increased resources based on observed usage resources=client.V1ResourceRequirements( requests={'cpu': '500m', 'memory': '2Gi'}, # Was 1Gi limits={'cpu': '2', 'memory': '4Gi'} # Was 2Gi ) ), # NEW: Add autoscaling based on CPU/memory min_replicas=2, # Was 1 max_replicas=5, # Was 3 scale_target=80, # CPU utilization target scale_metric="cpu" ) ) ) ```

docs.title: E. Creating New Notebooks for New Use Cases
docs.text:
When to Create: - Lightspeed query reveals gap in coverage - New anomaly pattern discovered - New remediation strategy needed

Example: Creating a memory leak detection notebook

```bash

docs.title: Create new notebook
docs.text:
touch notebooks/05-end-to-end-scenarios/memory-leak-detection-and-remediation.ipynb ```

Notebook Structure: ```python

docs.title: HEADER: Memory Leak Detection and Remediation
Purpose: Detect gradual memory leaks using trend analysis
Trigger: Lightspeed query "memory leak" returned no results
Collect memory metrics with fine-grained timestamps
docs.text: memory_metrics = query_prometheus( query='container_memory_usage_bytes{pod=~".predictor."}', start_time='7d', step='1m' )

docs.title: Use linear regression to detect gradual increases
docs.text: from sklearn.linear_regression import LinearRegression model = LinearRegression()

docs.title: Submit to coordination engine with specific action
docs.text: coordination_engine_client.submit_anomaly({ 'type': 'memory_leak', 'severity': 'high', 'recommended_action': 'rolling_restart', 'details': { 'leak_rate_mb_per_hour': 8.5, 'projected_oom_time': '4 hours' } })

docs.title: Step 5: Deploy and Validate Updates
docs.text: Option 1: NotebookValidationJob (Automated) ```bash

docs.title: Check existing validation jobs
docs.text: oc get notebookvalidationjob -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Trigger re-validation (ArgoCD will detect changes)
docs.text: git add notebooks/ git commit -m "feat: improve memory leak detection based on Lightspeed feedback" git push

docs.title: Wait for NotebookValidationJobs to complete
docs.text:
oc get notebookvalidationjob -n self-healing-platform -w ```

Option 2: Manual Execution in Workbench ```bash

docs.title: Port-forward to workbench
docs.text: oc port-forward self-healing-workbench-0 8888:8888 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Verify models saved to /mnt/models/
docs.text:
```

Validation Checklist: - [ ] Models trained successfully without errors - [ ] Models saved to correct PVC path (/mnt/models/{model-name}/) - [ ] InferenceService pods restarted and ready - [ ] Coordination engine accepts new anomaly submissions - [ ] Metrics exported to Prometheus

docs.title: Step 6: Re-test with OpenShift Lightspeed
docs.text:
Verification Queries: ``` "Are there any memory leaks now?" [Expected: Lightspeed now detects the gradual memory leak]

"What is the confidence score for the memory leak detection?" [Expected: Shows improved confidence with new model]

"Trigger remediation for the memory leak" [Expected: Successfully initiates rolling restart] ```

Check MCP Server Logs: ```bash

docs.title: Verify improved performance
docs.text: oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform \ --since=1h | grep "analyze-anomalies"

docs.title: Example 1: Improving False Positive Rate
docs.text:
Initial Situation: ``` Lightspeed Query: "Show me anomalies from last hour" Response: "47 anomalies detected, 32 critical"

[Reality: Only 3 were real issues - 91% false positive rate!] ```

Actions Taken: 1. Updated 02-anomaly-detection/01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb - Reduced contamination from 0.1 to 0.03 - Added feature normalization - Increased min_samples_split to reduce noise

  1. Updated 03-self-healing-logic/coordination-engine-integration.ipynb
  2. Added confidence score threshold (>0.85)
  3. Implemented anomaly correlation logic

  4. Added historical validation

  5. Retrained models via NotebookValidationJob

Result: ``` Lightspeed Query: "Show me anomalies from last hour" Response: "4 anomalies detected, 2 critical"

[Reality: All 4 were real issues - 0% false positive rate!] ```

docs.title: Example 2: Adding Proactive Scaling
docs.text:
Initial Situation: ``` Lightspeed Query: "Why did the predictive-analytics pod crash?" Response: "OOMKilled due to memory limit. Occurred at 14:32 UTC."

[Reactive response - pod already crashed] ```

Actions Taken: 1. Created 05-end-to-end-scenarios/proactive-resource-scaling.ipynb - Predict memory exhaustion 15 minutes in advance - Train LSTM model on memory growth patterns - Integrate with coordination engine for proactive scaling

  1. Updated 04-model-serving/kserve-model-deployment.ipynb
  2. Added HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) configuration

  3. Increased memory limits based on observed peaks

  4. Updated MCP server to expose predict-resource-exhaustion tool

Result: ``` Lightspeed Query: "Are there any resource issues?" Response: "Proactive scaling recommendation: predictive-analytics pod will reach memory limit in 12 minutes. Scaling action scheduled for 14:20 UTC."

[Proactive response - prevents crash] ```

docs.title: 1. Version Your Notebooks
docs.text: bash git add notebooks/ git commit -m "feat: improve anomaly detection based on user query feedback" git tag v1.2.0-anomaly-detection git push --tags

docs.title: 2. Document Changes
docs.text: Add metadata to notebooks: ```python

docs.title: 3. Track Metrics
docs.text: Monitor improvement over time: ```python

docs.title: Add to monitoring notebook
docs.text: metrics = { 'false_positive_rate': 0.09, # Was 0.91 'false_negative_rate': 0.05, # Was 0.15 'avg_response_time_ms': 234, # Was 1200 'user_satisfaction': 4.2, # Was 2.8 'remediation_success_rate': 0.92 # Was 0.67 } ```

docs.title: 4. Automate the Loop
docs.text: ```yaml

docs.title: Add to tekton/pipelines/
docs.text: apiVersion: tekton.dev/v1 kind: Pipeline metadata: name: lightspeed-feedback-pipeline spec: tasks: - name: analyze-mcp-logs - name: identify-gaps - name: update-notebooks - name: retrain-models - name: validate-improvements ```

docs.title: Issue: Notebook changes not reflected in MCP responses
docs.text: Check: ```bash

docs.title: 1. Verify models saved correctly
docs.text: oc exec -it self-healing-workbench-0 -- ls -la /mnt/models/predictive-analytics/

docs.title: 2. Restart InferenceService predictor
docs.text: oc delete pod -n self-healing-platform -l serving.kserve.io/inferenceservice=predictive-analytics

docs.title: 3. Check MCP server is using new models
docs.text: oc logs deployment/cluster-health-mcp-server -n self-healing-platform | grep "Model loaded" ```

docs.title: Issue: NotebookValidationJob fails after notebook update
docs.text: Check: ```bash

docs.title: View validation job logs
docs.text: oc logs -n self-healing-platform -l job-name=01-isolation-forest-implementation-validation

docs.title: - Missing dependencies: Add to notebook-validator image
Related Documentation

docs.text:
1. Start Simple: Pick one query, identify one gap, update one notebook
  2. Measure Impact: Track metrics before and after updates
  3. Iterate: Repeat the cycle weekly or after major incidents
  4. Share Learning: Document patterns in ADRs and notebooks
