✅ Success check: You should see "✅ Connected to Prometheus"

docs.title: 3.2 Query CPU Metrics
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Get 7 days of CPU usage across all pods
docs.text:
query = 'sum(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="self-healing-platform"}[5m])) by (pod)' end_time = datetime.now() start_time = end_time - timedelta(days=7)

print(f"Querying Prometheus from {start_time} to {end_time}...")

metric_data = prom.get_metric_range_data( metric_name=query, start_time=start_time, end_time=end_time, chunk_size=timedelta(hours=1) )

print(f"✅ Retrieved {len(metric_data)} time series") ```

What's happening: This queries Prometheus for CPU usage patterns over the last 7 days. This real data will train your anomaly detector.

docs.title: 3.3 Process Data into DataFrame
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Cell 5: Convert to DataFrame
docs.text:
data_points = []

for metric in metric_data: pod_name = metric['metric'].get('pod', 'unknown') for value in metric['values']: timestamp, cpu_usage = value data_points.append({ 'timestamp': pd.to_datetime(timestamp, unit='s'), 'pod': pod_name, 'cpu_usage': float(cpu_usage) })

df = pd.DataFrame(data_points)

print(f"✅ Processed {len(df)} data points") print(f"\nSample data:") print(df.head()) ```

✅ Success check: You should see a DataFrame with columns: timestamp, pod, cpu_usage

docs.title: Step 4: Train the Anomaly Detection Model
4.1 Prepare Features
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Create time-based features
docs.text: df['hour'] = df['timestamp'].dt.hour df['day_of_week'] = df['timestamp'].dt.dayofweek

docs.title: Create rolling statistics (5-minute windows)
docs.text: df = df.sort_values('timestamp') df['cpu_rolling_mean'] = df.groupby('pod')['cpu_usage'].transform( lambda x: x.rolling(window=5, min_periods=1).mean() ) df['cpu_rolling_std'] = df.groupby('pod')['cpu_usage'].transform( lambda x: x.rolling(window=5, min_periods=1).std() )

docs.title: Fill NaN values
docs.text: df = df.fillna(0)

docs.title: Select features for training
docs.text:
features = ['cpu_usage', 'hour', 'day_of_week', 'cpu_rolling_mean', 'cpu_rolling_std'] X = df[features]

print(f"✅ Features prepared: {features}") print(f"Training data shape: {X.shape}") ```

docs.title: 4.2 Train Isolation Forest Model
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Isolation Forest is great for anomaly detection
docs.text:
model = IsolationForest( n_estimators=100, # Number of trees contamination=0.1, # Expected proportion of anomalies (10%) random_state=42, # Reproducibility n_jobs=-1 # Use all CPU cores )

print("Training Isolation Forest model...") model.fit(X)

print("✅ Model trained successfully!") print(f"Model has {model.n_estimators} trees") ```

What's happening: The Isolation Forest algorithm learns normal CPU usage patterns. It will later identify deviations from these patterns as anomalies.

docs.title: 4.3 Test the Model Locally
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Cell 8: Make predictions on training data
docs.text: predictions = model.predict(X) anomaly_scores = model.decision_function(X)

docs.title: -1 = anomaly, 1 = normal
docs.text:
num_anomalies = (predictions == -1).sum() num_normal = (predictions == 1).sum()

print(f"✅ Predictions complete:") print(f" Normal points: {num_normal}") print(f" Anomalies detected: {num_anomalies}") print(f" Anomaly rate: {num_anomalies / len(predictions) * 100:.2f}%")

docs.title: Show sample anomalies
docs.text:
anomaly_df = df[predictions == -1][['timestamp', 'pod', 'cpu_usage']].head(10) print(f"\nSample anomalies detected:") print(anomaly_df) ```

✅ Success check: You should see anomalies detected (around 10% of data points)

docs.title: 5.1 Create Model Directory
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Cell 9: Save model to persistent storage
docs.text:
model_dir = "/opt/app-root/src/models/my-first-model" os.makedirs(model_dir, exist_ok=True)

model_path = os.path.join(model_dir, "model.joblib") metadata_path = os.path.join(model_dir, "metadata.txt")

print(f"✅ Created model directory: {model_dir}") ```

docs.title: 5.2 Serialize the Model
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Cell 10: Save model with joblib
docs.text: joblib.dump(model, model_path)

docs.title: Save metadata
docs.text:
metadata = f"""Model: Isolation Forest Anomaly Detector Trained: {datetime.now().isoformat()} Features: {features} Training samples: {len(X)} Anomalies detected: {num_anomalies} Expected contamination: 10% """

with open(metadata_path, 'w') as f: f.write(metadata)

print(f"✅ Model saved to: {model_path}") print(f"✅ Metadata saved to: {metadata_path}") print(f"\nModel size: {os.path.getsize(model_path) / 1024:.2f} KB") ```

✅ Success check: Model file should be created (~50-100 KB)

docs.title: 6.1 Create InferenceService Manifest
docs.text:
Switch to terminal (File → New → Terminal) and create the InferenceService YAML:

```bash cat > /tmp/my-first-inferenceservice.yaml <<'EOF' apiVersion: serving.kserve.io/v1beta1 kind: InferenceService metadata: name: my-first-model namespace: self-healing-platform annotations: serving.kserve.io/deploymentMode: RawDeployment spec: predictor: model: modelFormat: name: sklearn storageUri: pvc://model-storage/my-first-model resources: requests: cpu: "100m" memory: "256Mi" limits: cpu: "500m" memory: "512Mi" EOF

echo "✅ InferenceService manifest created" ```

docs.title: 6.2 Deploy to Kubernetes
docs.text:
bash oc apply -f /tmp/my-first-inferenceservice.yaml

Expected output: inferenceservice.serving.kserve.io/my-first-model created

docs.title: 6.3 Wait for Model to Deploy
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Watch deployment progress
docs.text:
oc get inferenceservice my-first-model -n self-healing-platform -w ```

Wait for: NAME URL READY PREV LATEST ... my-first-model http://my-first-model.self-healing-platform... True 100

Press Ctrl+C when READY shows True.

⏱️ This takes 2-3 minutes - KServe is: 1. Creating a predictor pod 2. Loading your model 3. Starting the inference server 4. Health checking the endpoint

docs.title: 7.1 Get the Inference Endpoint
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Get the route URL
docs.text: MODEL_URL=$(oc get route my-first-model -n self-healing-platform -o jsonpath='{.spec.host}') echo "Model endpoint: https://${MODEL_URL}" ```

docs.title: 7.2 Create Test Data
docs.text:
Back in your notebook:

```python

docs.title: Cell 11: Prepare test inference data
docs.text: test_data = { "instances": [ [0.5, 10, 1, 0.45, 0.05], # Normal CPU usage at 10am on Monday [2.5, 3, 6, 2.3, 0.8], # High CPU at 3am on Saturday (anomaly) [0.3, 14, 2, 0.35, 0.02] # Normal usage at 2pm on Tuesday ] }

docs.title: Features: cpu_usage, hour, day_of_week, cpu_rolling_mean, cpu_rolling_std
docs.text: print("Test instances:") for i, instance in enumerate(test_data["instances"]): print(f" {i+1}. CPU={instance[0]}, Hour={instance[1]}, Day={instance[2]}") ```

docs.title: 7.3 Make Predictions via HTTP
docs.text: ```python

docs.title: Cell 12: Call the inference endpoint
docs.text: import requests import json

docs.title: You'll need the MODEL_URL from the terminal above
docs.text:
MODEL_URL = "my-first-model-self-healing-platform.apps.cluster-xxxxx.xxxxx.example.com" endpoint = f"https://{MODEL_URL}/v1/models/my-first-model:predict"

response = requests.post( endpoint, json=test_data, headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"}, verify=False # Skip SSL verification for demo )

if response.status_code == 200: predictions = response.json() print("✅ Predictions received:") print(json.dumps(predictions, indent=2))

# Interpret results
for i, pred in enumerate(predictions['predictions']):
    label = "🔴 ANOMALY" if pred == -1 else "🟢 Normal"
    print(f"Instance {i+1}: {label}")

else: print(f"❌ Request failed: {response.status_code}") print(response.text) ```

✅ Success check: You should see predictions like: Instance 1: 🟢 Normal Instance 2: 🔴 ANOMALY Instance 3: 🟢 Normal

Congratulations! 🎉 Your model is now serving predictions via HTTP!

docs.title: 8.1 Check Predictor Pods
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: See the running predictor pod
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform -l serving.kserve.io/inferenceservice=my-first-model

docs.title: View model server logs
docs.text: oc logs -n self-healing-platform -l serving.kserve.io/inferenceservice=my-first-model -c kserve-container --tail=50 ```

docs.title: 8.2 View Model Metrics (Optional)
docs.text:
If Prometheus ServiceMonitor is configured:

```python

docs.title: Cell 13: Query model serving metrics
docs.text:
query = 'sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="my-first-model"}[5m]))' result = prom.custom_query(query=query)

if result: print(f"✅ Model request rate: {result[0]['value'][1]} requests/sec") else: print("ℹ️ Metrics not yet available (make a few more requests)") ```

docs.title: What You've Learned
docs.text:
In this tutorial, you:

✅ Collected real data from Prometheus ✅ Engineered features for time-series anomaly detection ✅ Trained an Isolation Forest model on cluster CPU metrics ✅ Serialized and stored the model ✅ Deployed to KServe as a scalable inference service ✅ Made predictions via HTTP REST API ✅ Monitored model deployment and logs

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Now that you have a working model, try:

docs.title: 1. Improve the Model
docs.text:
• Add more features: Memory usage, network I/O, disk I/O
  • Tune hyperparameters: Adjust n_estimators, contamination
  • Try different algorithms: LSTM, Autoencoders, Prophet
  • Cross-validate: Split data into train/test sets

See: Workbench Development Guide

docs.title: 2. Automate Model Training
docs.text:
• Set up Tekton pipelines: Auto-retrain on new data
  • Schedule periodic updates: Daily or weekly retraining
  • Version your models: Keep track of model iterations

See: ADR-053: Tekton Pipelines for Model Training

docs.title: 3. Integrate with Self-Healing
docs.text:
• Connect to coordination engine: Send anomaly alerts
  • Trigger remediations: Auto-scale pods, restart services
  • Build feedback loops: Improve model with remediation outcomes

See: ADR-002: Hybrid Self-Healing Approach

docs.title: 4. Production Deployment
docs.text:
• Add canary rollouts: Gradual model deployment
  • Implement A/B testing: Compare model versions
  • Set up alerting: Monitor prediction drift
  • Enable autoscaling: Scale based on traffic

See: ADR-040: Extensible KServe Model Registry

docs.title: Issue: "Connection to Prometheus failed"
docs.text:
Solution: Check if Prometheus is accessible:

bash oc get svc -n openshift-monitoring prometheus-k8s

If missing, you may need cluster-admin to access monitoring namespace.

docs.title: Issue: "InferenceService not becoming Ready"
docs.text:
Check predictor pod logs:

bash oc get pods -n self-healing-platform -l serving.kserve.io/inferenceservice=my-first-model oc logs <predictor-pod-name> -c kserve-container

Common causes: - Model file not found (check PVC mount) - Insufficient resources (increase CPU/memory limits) - Model format mismatch (verify sklearn version)

docs.title: Issue: "Predictions return errors"
docs.text:
Verify input shape:

```python

docs.title: Model expects 5 features: cpu_usage, hour, day_of_week, cpu_rolling_mean, cpu_rolling_std
Each instance must be a list of 5 numbers
docs.text: test_data = { "instances": [ [0.5, 10, 1, 0.45, 0.05] # ✅ Correct: 5 features # [0.5, 10, 1] ❌ Wrong: only 3 features ] } ```

docs.title: Clean Up
docs.text:
When you're done experimenting:

```bash

docs.title: Delete the InferenceService
docs.text: oc delete inferenceservice my-first-model -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Delete model files (from notebook)
docs.text: !rm -rf /opt/app-root/src/models/my-first-model ```

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text:
• KServe Documentation - Official KServe docs
  • Scikit-learn Isolation Forest - Algorithm details
  • ADR-004: KServe for Model Serving - Architecture decision
  • ADR-039: User-Deployed KServe Models - Model deployment patterns

Questions or stuck? Open an issue: https://github.com/KubeHeal/openshift-aiops-platform/issues

Tutorial last updated: 2026-05-18 Tested on: OpenShift 4.20, RHOAI 2.22.2, KServe 1.36.1

docs.title: Getting Started with OpenShift AIOps Platform
docs.text: Welcome to the OpenShift AIOps Platform! This tutorial will guide you through accessing the Self-Healing Platform workbench and running your first AI/ML experiments.

docs.title: About Red Hat OpenShift AI (RHODS)
docs.text:
Red Hat OpenShift AI (formerly Red Hat OpenShift Data Science) is an enterprise-grade machine learning platform built on Kubernetes. It provides:

  • Workbenches: Pre-configured Jupyter notebook environments for data science development
  • Model Training: GPU-accelerated training with PyTorch, TensorFlow, and other frameworks
  • Model Serving: Deploy trained models as scalable inference endpoints
  • Data Management: Integrated data storage and feature management
  • Collaboration: Multi-user support with role-based access control
  • Hardware Acceleration: GPU and specialized hardware support for ML workloads

The Self-Healing Platform leverages RHODS to provide a complete AI/ML development environment for building anomaly detection models and self-healing algorithms.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text: By the end of this tutorial, you will: - Access the RHODS (Red Hat OpenShift AI) workbench - Verify your development environment (Python, PyTorch, GPU) - Clone the Self-Healing Platform repository - Run your first anomaly detection experiment - Understand the platform's key components and architecture

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before you begin, ensure you have: - OpenShift Cluster Access: Access to an OpenShift 4.18+ cluster with Red Hat OpenShift AI installed - CLI Tools: oc CLI tool installed and configured on your local machine - Credentials: Valid OpenShift credentials with access to the self-healing-platform namespace - Basic Knowledge: Familiarity with Python, Jupyter notebooks, and Git - Browser: A modern web browser for accessing the RHODS dashboard

docs.title: Step 1: Access the Red Hat OpenShift AI Dashboard
1.1 Open the RHODS Dashboard
docs.text:
The RHODS dashboard is your gateway to the Self-Healing Platform workbench. This is the central hub for managing your data science workbenches, notebooks, and ML projects.

Navigate to the RHODS dashboard:

https://rhods-dashboard-redhat-ods-applications.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/

You should see the RHODS login page. Authenticate using your OpenShift credentials.

What is the RHODS Dashboard?

The RHODS dashboard provides: - Workbench Management: Start, stop, and configure Jupyter notebook environments - Project Organization: Organize your work into data science projects - Resource Monitoring: Track CPU, memory, and GPU usage - Model Registry: Manage and version your trained models - Connections: Configure connections to data sources and external services

docs.title: 1.2 Verify Workbench Status
docs.text:
Once logged in, you'll see the RHODS dashboard with available workbenches. A workbench in RHODS is a containerized Jupyter notebook environment with pre-installed libraries and tools for data science work.

Verify that the Self-Healing Workbench is running:

  1. Look for "self-healing-workbench-dev" in the workbench list
  2. Check that the status shows Running (green indicator)
  3. If not running, click the Start button to launch it
  4. Wait for the workbench to initialize (typically 1-2 minutes)

Expected Result: You should see a workbench with status "Running" and a Connect button available.

About the Self-Healing Workbench

The Self-Healing Workbench is configured with: - Base Image: PyTorch 2025.1 with CUDA support - Pre-installed Libraries: - PyTorch 2.x for deep learning - NumPy, Pandas, Scikit-learn for data processing - Prometheus client for metrics collection - Kubernetes client for cluster interaction - Storage: - 5Gi persistent volume for data and notebooks - 10Gi ReadWriteMany volume for shared model artifacts - GPU Support: NVIDIA GPU acceleration (if available in cluster)

docs.title: 2.1 Terminal Access (Recommended)
docs.text:
For immediate productivity, use terminal access via the oc CLI:

```bash

docs.title: Verify the workbench pod is running
docs.text: oc get pods -n self-healing-platform | grep workbench

docs.title: self-healing-workbench-dev-0   2/2     Running     0          10m
docs.text:
```

Connect to the workbench terminal:

```bash

docs.title: Connect to the workbench container
docs.text:
oc exec -it self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -c self-healing-workbench \ -n self-healing-platform -- /bin/bash ```

You should see a prompt like: (base) 1001@self-healing-workbench-dev-0:/opt/app-root/src$

docs.title: 2.2 Web Interface Access (Alternative)
docs.text:
You can also access the workbench through the RHODS web interface:

  1. Click the Connect button next to the workbench in the RHODS dashboard
  2. Wait for JupyterLab to load (this may take 30-60 seconds)
  3. You'll see the JupyterLab interface with a file browser on the left

Note: The web interface may have configuration complexities with the PyTorch base image. If you experience issues, use terminal access instead.

docs.title: 3.1 Check Python and PyTorch
docs.text:
Verify that your development environment is properly configured:

```bash

docs.title: Check Python version
docs.text: python --version

docs.title: Check PyTorch installation
docs.text: python -c "import torch; print(f'PyTorch: {torch.version}')"

docs.title: Check available ML libraries
docs.text: pip list | grep -E "(torch|numpy|pandas|scikit-learn|prometheus)" ```

docs.title: 3.2 Verify GPU Access (Optional)
docs.text:
If GPU nodes are available in your cluster:

```bash

docs.title: Check CUDA availability
docs.text: python -c "import torch; print(f'CUDA available: {torch.cuda.is_available()}')"

docs.title: Check GPU count
docs.text: python -c "import torch; print(f'GPU count: {torch.cuda.device_count()}')"

docs.title: Test GPU tensor creation
docs.text: python -c " import torch if torch.cuda.is_available(): x = torch.randn(3, 3).cuda() print(f'GPU tensor created: {x.device}') else: print('CUDA not available - CPU mode') " ```

docs.title: 3.3 Verify Persistent Storage
docs.text:
Check that your persistent volumes are mounted:

```bash

docs.title: List data directory (5Gi persistent volume)
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/data

docs.title: List models directory (10Gi ReadWriteMany persistent volume)
docs.text: ls -la /opt/app-root/src/models

docs.title: Create working directories
docs.text: mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/data/notebooks mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/data/datasets mkdir -p /opt/app-root/src/models/checkpoints ```

docs.title: 4.1 Clone the Project
docs.text:
Inside the workbench terminal, clone the Self-Healing Platform repository:

```bash

docs.title: Clone the repository
docs.text: git clone https://gitea-with-admin-gitea.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/user1/openshift-aiops-platform.git

docs.title: Navigate to the project directory
docs.text: cd openshift-aiops-platform

docs.title: Verify the clone
docs.text:
ls -la ```

You should see directories like: - docs/ - Documentation and ADRs - src/ - Source code - k8s/ - Kubernetes manifests - notebooks/ - Jupyter notebooks (create if needed)

docs.title: 4.2 Install Project Dependencies
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Install project requirements
docs.text: pip install --user -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Install additional ML libraries for anomaly detection
docs.text: pip install --user \ statsmodels>=0.14.0 \ prophet>=1.1.4 \ tslearn>=0.6.0 \ pyod>=1.1.0 \ xgboost>=2.0.0 \ prometheus-client>=0.17.0 ```

docs.title: 5.1 Create a Simple Anomaly Detection Script
docs.text:
Create a test script to verify everything works:

```bash

docs.title: Create a test script
docs.text: cat > test_anomaly_detection.py << 'EOF' import torch import numpy as np from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

docs.title: Generate sample time series data
docs.text: np.random.seed(42) normal_data = np.random.normal(100, 10, 1000) anomalies = np.array([150, 160, 155]) # Anomalous values data = np.concatenate([normal_data, anomalies])

docs.title: Standardize the data
docs.text: scaler = StandardScaler() scaled_data = scaler.fit_transform(data.reshape(-1, 1))

docs.title: Simple anomaly detection using z-score
docs.text:
z_scores = np.abs(scaled_data) threshold = 3 anomaly_indices = np.where(z_scores > threshold)[0]

print(f"Total data points: {len(data)}") print(f"Detected anomalies: {len(anomaly_indices)}") print(f"Anomaly indices: {anomaly_indices}") print(f"PyTorch available: {torch.cuda.is_available()}") print("✓ Environment verification successful!") EOF

docs.title: Run the test script
docs.text:
python test_anomaly_detection.py ```

Expected Output: Total data points: 1003 Detected anomalies: 3 Anomaly indices: [1000 1001 1002] PyTorch available: False (or True if GPU available) ✓ Environment verification successful!

docs.title: 6.1 Review Architecture Documentation
docs.text:
Understand the platform design:

```bash

docs.title: Read the architecture overview
docs.text: cat docs/explanation/architecture-overview.md

docs.title: Review key ADRs
docs.text: cat docs/adrs/002-hybrid-self-healing-approach.md cat docs/adrs/011-self-healing-workbench-base-image.md ```

docs.title: 6.2 Explore Example Notebooks
docs.text:
Check out example notebooks in the repository:

```bash

docs.title: List available notebooks
docs.text: find . -name "*.ipynb" -type f

docs.title: Or create your own notebook directory
docs.text: mkdir -p notebooks/experiments cd notebooks/experiments ```

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: In this tutorial, you learned how to: - ✓ Access the RHODS dashboard and workbench - ✓ Verify your development environment (Python, PyTorch, GPU) - ✓ Clone the Self-Healing Platform repository - ✓ Install project dependencies - ✓ Run a simple anomaly detection experiment - ✓ Understand the platform structure

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you have a working environment, explore these resources:

  1. Workbench Development Guide - Deep dive into developing self-healing algorithms
  2. How to Access Workbench - Detailed access instructions and troubleshooting
  3. Architecture Overview - Understand the system design
  4. ADR Reference - Review architectural decisions

docs.title: Issue: Workbench Pod Not Running
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Check pod status
docs.text: oc describe pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform

docs.title: Check events
docs.text: oc get events -n self-healing-platform --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

docs.title: Restart the workbench
docs.text: oc delete pod self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -n self-healing-platform ```

docs.title: Issue: Git Clone Fails
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Configure git credentials
docs.text: git config --global user.name "Your Name" git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"

docs.title: Use token authentication if needed
docs.text: git clone https://username:token@gitea-server/repo.git ```

docs.title: Issue: Package Installation Fails
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Use user installation
docs.text: pip install --user package_name

docs.title: Clear pip cache
docs.text: pip cache purge

docs.title: Check pip configuration
docs.text: pip config list ```

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
If you encounter issues:

  1. Check the How-To Guides for detailed troubleshooting
  2. Review the ADRs for architectural context
  3. Check pod logs: oc logs self-healing-workbench-dev-0 -c self-healing-workbench -n self-healing-platform
  4. Contact the platform team for assistance

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This guide walks you through using the Self-Healing Platform's AI/ML workbench for developing anomaly detection models and self-healing algorithms. The workbench uses a PyTorch 2025.1 base image with GPU acceleration support.

This is a comprehensive end-to-end guide covering: 1. Workbench Setup - Access and environment verification 2. Model Development - Anomaly detection and predictive analytics 3. Model Serving - Deploy models with KServe 4. MCP Server Integration - Connect to OpenShift Lightspeed 5. Self-Healing Workflows - Implement remediation logic 6. Monitoring & Metrics - Prometheus integration

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to the OpenShift cluster
  • oc CLI tool configured
  • Basic knowledge of Python, Jupyter, and Git
  • GPU node available (optional but recommended)

docs.title: Quick Start (In RHODS Workbench)
docs.text: Assumption: You're already in the RHODS workbench at: https://self-healing-workbench-dev-self-healing-platform.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/notebook/self-healing-platform/self-healing-workbench-dev/lab

docs.title: Step 1: Open Terminal in JupyterLab
docs.text:
In the JupyterLab interface: 1. Click File → New → Terminal 2. Or use the Terminal icon in the launcher

You'll now have a terminal in /opt/app-root/src/

docs.title: Step 2: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
In the terminal, clone the project repository:

```bash

docs.title: Clone the Self-Healing Platform repository
docs.text: git clone https://gitea-with-admin-gitea.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/user1/openshift-aiops-platform.git

docs.title: Navigate to the project directory
docs.text: cd openshift-aiops-platform

docs.title: Verify the clone
docs.text: ls -la ```

docs.title: Step 3: Run Environment Setup Notebook ⭐ IMPORTANT
docs.text:
Before running any other notebooks, you MUST run the setup notebook:

  1. In the file browser, navigate to: openshift-aiops-platform/notebooks/00-setup/
  2. Double-click: environment-setup.ipynb
  3. Click "Run All" button
  4. Wait for all cells to complete
  5. Review the setup summary report

This notebook will: - ✅ Verify Python and PyTorch installation - ✅ Check GPU availability - ✅ Verify persistent storage volumes - ✅ Test required dependencies - ✅ Create necessary directories - ✅ Generate setup summary report

Time: 5-10 minutes

What to look for: - Python 3.11+ ✓ - PyTorch 2025.1 ✓ - CUDA Available (True or False - both OK) - All dependencies installed ✓ - Data and Models volumes accessible ✓

docs.title: Step 4: Address Any Issues (If Needed)
docs.text:
If the setup notebook shows missing dependencies:

```bash

docs.title: Install additional packages (if needed)
docs.text:
pip install --user \ statsmodels>=0.14.0 \ prophet>=1.1.4 \ tslearn>=0.6.0 \ pyod>=1.1.0 \ xgboost>=2.0.0 \ lightgbm>=4.0.0 \ prometheus-client>=0.17.0 \ kubernetes>=28.0.0 \ kserve>=0.11.0 \ seaborn>=0.12.0 \ plotly>=5.17.0 \ bokeh>=3.0.0 \ networkx>=3.0 \ pyyaml>=6.0 \ requests>=2.31.0 ```

Note: Most dependencies are pre-installed in PyTorch 2025.1 image.

docs.title: Working with Jupyter Notebooks
docs.text:
You're already in JupyterLab! Here's how to work with notebooks:

  1. Navigate to notebooks directory:
  2. In the file browser on the left, navigate to: openshift-aiops-platform/notebooks/

  3. Or use the terminal: cd openshift-aiops-platform/notebooks/

  4. Open a notebook:

  5. Double-click any .ipynb file to open it

  6. Or right-click → Open With → Notebook

  7. Run cells:

  8. Click a cell and press Shift+Enter to run it
  9. Or use the Run button in the toolbar

  10. Use "Run All" to execute the entire notebook

  11. Create new notebooks:

  12. Right-click in file browser → New → Notebook
  13. Select Python 3 kernel
  14. Start coding!

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
The repository contains:

openshift-aiops-platform/ ├── docs/ │ ├── adrs/ # Architectural Decision Records │ ├── tutorials/ # Tutorial guides (this file!) │ ├── NOTEBOOK-EXECUTION-READY.md # Start here! │ ├── NOTEBOOK-EXECUTION-GUIDE.md # Detailed instructions │ └── NOTEBOOK-EXECUTION-CHECKLIST.md # Track progress ├── notebooks/ │ ├── 01-data-collection/ # Phase 1: Data Collection (5 notebooks) │ ├── 02-anomaly-detection/ # Phase 2: Anomaly Detection (4 notebooks) │ ├── 03-self-healing-logic/ # Phase 3: Self-Healing Logic (4 notebooks) │ ├── 04-model-serving/ # Phase 4: Model Serving (3 notebooks) │ ├── 05-end-to-end-scenarios/ # Phase 5: End-to-End Scenarios (4 notebooks) │ ├── 06-mcp-lightspeed-integration/ # Phase 6: MCP & Lightspeed (3 notebooks) │ ├── 07-monitoring-operations/ # Phase 7: Monitoring & Operations (3 notebooks) │ ├── 08-advanced-scenarios/ # Phase 8: Advanced Scenarios (4 notebooks) │ ├── utils/ # Shared utilities │ └── README.md # Notebook documentation ├── src/coordination-engine/ # Self-healing coordination logic ├── k8s/ # Kubernetes manifests └── models/ # Trained models (persistent storage)

docs.title: Working with Persistent Storage
docs.text:
The workbench has access to persistent volumes:

  • Data Volume (/opt/app-root/src/data): Store datasets and training data (20GB)
  • Models Volume (/opt/app-root/src/models): Store trained models (50GB, shared across pods)

In your notebooks:

```python import pandas as pd import joblib

docs.title: Save training data
docs.text: df = pd.DataFrame(...) df.to_parquet('/opt/app-root/src/data/my_dataset.parquet')

docs.title: Load training data

docs.title: End-to-End Notebook Tutorials
docs.text: Follow these notebooks in order to build a complete self-healing platform workflow. The notebooks are organized in the notebooks/ directory and follow a structured learning path.

docs.title: 1.1 Prometheus Metrics Collection
docs.text:
File: notebooks/01-data-collection/prometheus-metrics-collection.ipynb ✅ EXISTS

Objectives: - Collect Prometheus metrics from OpenShift cluster - Process and normalize metrics - Store in feature store - Validate data quality

Key Topics: - Prometheus query API - Time series data handling - Feature engineering - Data validation

docs.title: 1.2 OpenShift Events Analysis
docs.text:
File: notebooks/01-data-collection/openshift-events-analysis.ipynb ✅ EXISTS

Key Topics: - Kubernetes event API - Event parsing and filtering - Pattern recognition - Event correlation

docs.title: 🚀 Getting Started with Notebooks (In Workbench)
docs.text: You're already in the workbench at: https://self-healing-workbench-dev-self-healing-platform.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/notebook/self-healing-platform/self-healing-workbench-dev/lab

docs.title: Quick Start Path
docs.text:
1. Open a terminal in JupyterLab:
  2. Click File → New → Terminal
  3. Or click the Terminal icon in the launcher

  4. You'll be in /opt/app-root/src/

  5. Clone the Repository (if not already cloned): bash cd /opt/app-root/src git clone https://gitea-with-admin-gitea.apps.cluster-dzqpc.dzqpc.sandbox29.opentlc.com/user1/openshift-aiops-platform.git cd openshift-aiops-platform

  6. ⭐ RUN SETUP NOTEBOOK FIRST (5-10 minutes):

  7. Navigate to: openshift-aiops-platform/notebooks/00-setup/
  8. Double-click: environment-setup.ipynb
  9. Click "Run All" button
  10. Review the setup summary report

  11. This is REQUIRED before running other notebooks!

  12. Start with Phase 1 - Data Collection:

  13. Navigate to: openshift-aiops-platform/notebooks/01-data-collection/
  14. Double-click: prometheus-metrics-collection.ipynb
  15. Click "Run All" or run cells with Shift+Enter

  16. Review outputs and verify data collection works

  17. Progress to Phase 2 - Anomaly Detection:

  18. Navigate to: notebooks/02-anomaly-detection/
  19. Open: 01-isolation-forest-implementation.ipynb
  20. Train your first anomaly detection model

  21. Model will be saved to /opt/app-root/src/models/

  22. Continue through all 8 phases:

  23. Follow: docs/NOTEBOOK-EXECUTION-CHECKLIST.md
  24. Check off each notebook as you complete it

  25. Estimated total time: 18-24 hours (plus 5-10 min setup)

  26. Track your progress:

  27. Use: docs/NOTEBOOK-EXECUTION-CHECKLIST.md (in the repo)
  28. Or create your own execution log in a notebook

docs.title: Tips for Success
docs.text:
• Save your work: Use Ctrl+S frequently in notebooks
  • Check outputs: Review each notebook's output before moving to the next
  • Use persistent storage: Save models to /opt/app-root/src/models/
  • Monitor resources: Check GPU/CPU usage in terminal: nvidia-smi or top
  • Create checkpoints: Save intermediate results to persistent storage
  • Document findings: Add markdown cells with your observations

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Issue: Permission denied when writing files Solution: Use the persistent volumes (/opt/app-root/src/data or /opt/app-root/src/models)

Issue: GPU not available Solution: Check if GPU nodes are available: oc describe nodes | grep nvidia

Issue: Package installation fails Solution: Use pip install --user to install in user directory

docs.text:

  • Review the coordination engine source in src/coordination-engine/
  • Examine Kubernetes configurations in k8s/
